observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
	the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete and timely physical count of all LGU properties every year, prepare the corresponding RPCPPE and RPCLRN, and submit the same to the Accountant for reconciliation and to the Audit Team for verification not later than January 31 of each year; and
	the GSO to prepare the Report on the Local Road Network and update the Road Map with accurate and complete data on the Municipality’s Road Network Systems.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize personnel benefit contributions for GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth to its proper expense and liability accounts in the month to which the payroll pertains, rather than at the time of remittance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
	reassess the rationale for maintaining these time deposits and determine whether the funds can be redirected to more urgent and beneficial local development projects; and
	suspend further renewal of the time deposit accounts until proper authority is secured and all relevant documents are in place.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:
	coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan and the Office of the Municipal Mayor to secure proper documentation or obtain approval for the existing time deposit investments; and
	establish a reliable documentation and filing system to ensure all investment decisions are traceable, authorized, and compliant with applicable regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
	Municipal Budget Officer to identify potential funding sources for the construction of a covered storage facility for disaster equipment.
	Municipal Engineer, MDRRMO and GSO to prioritize the construction of a temporary covered storage facility or identify an existing sheltered area to protect the disaster-response vehicles and equipment from environmental damage, in order to prevent faster deterioration, breakdowns, and costly repairs or replacements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Agriculturist:
	Submit a justification for the acceptance and payment of the eighteen (18) Pump and Engines sets: Gasoline Engine, despite non-conformity with the technical specifications stated in the contract agreement; and
	Prepare and submit a document showing the comparison between the delivered Gasoline Engine and the Diesel Engine in terms of cost, performance, and efficiency.
We also recommended that the members of the BAC and the TWG explain and justify why the AMTEC test results were not required from the winning bidder during the procurement of the thirty-five (35) pump and engine sets.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant, henceforth, require the suppliers to submit an acceptable form of warranty security for the procurement of goods before payment is made, in order to protect the interests of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
	ensure that claims for reimbursement of transportation expenses by JO personnel are supported by an Approved Travel Request or Travel Order, Itinerary of Travel, and other documentary requirements prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
	henceforth, record the traveling expenses incurred by JO personnel under the Traveling Expenses account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the timely renewal of fidelity bonds for all AOs to safeguard government funds against loss and misappropriation; and
Require that all personnel handling government funds or property be sufficiently bonded in accordance with Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019.

We recommended that the Chairperson of the GFPS-TWG:
	seek the assistance of a gender analysis specialist/expert from the PCW or other relevant agencies to ensure proper implementation of the HGDG tool;
	conduct gender analysis of the attributed PPAs through the administration of the HGDG tool during the project identification and preparation of GAD AR to determine whether the attributed GAD PPAs entirely address gender related issues, and submit the corresponding scores; and
	establish, enhance, strengthen and maintain a gender responsive evaluation system to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Create a committee who will conduct an investigation on non-existing/missing PPEs and prepare a report thereon to determine the last known location/office on where the missing PPE items were issued/installed, the person accountable/ responsible for the loss, the circumstances of the loss, among others;

Submit to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and Supervising Auditor (SA) the request for authority to derecognize non-existing/missing PPEs for which accountability could no longer be pinpointed, together with the required supporting documents, particularly the certified copy of the report of investigation; and

Issue a written directive to the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to prepare the documentary requirements prescribed in COA Memorandum No. 92-751 dated February 21, 1992, for the filing at the Commission on Audit, of the request for relief from property accountability from the COA on the damaged Tanauan Public Market.  

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a reversing entry for the unauthorized derecognition of PPEs totaling ₱23,246,064.81.

Procurement of infrastructure projects, goods, supplies and/or services include its annual Procurement Plan (APP) and Supplemental Annual Procurement Plan (SAPP) as an attachment and part of the bidding documents for the succeeding opening of bids.
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) invited at least three observes in all phases of procurement activities.

All bidders were required to submit and use the amended Omnibus Sworn Statement, should then conform to the prescribed format provided by the GPPB and shall be mandatory upon all prospective bidders.
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) required the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid to submit its latest income and business tax returns within the last six months or preceding the date of bid submission and other relevant eligibility requirements.
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) required all the winning bidders to submit the identified documents such as the Construction and S-curve, Manpower Schedule, Construction Methods, Equipment Utilization Schedule, and/or other acceptable tools of project scheduling. Including the Construction Safety and Health Program (CSHP) duly approved by DOLE.

Observers were not invited during bid evaluation and Post-Qualification.


LCRB was not required to submit latest income and business tax returns on Notice of Post-Qualification.

but already resumed except for the water system at Barangay Bagumbayan. Whereas, the
CPDO will coordinate with the City Engineering Office for the early determination of possible
issues and concerns that may arise during project implementation to help mitigate delays.

We recommended that the City Mayor immediately demand the supplier to provide a
functional 5D facility hub that is in full conformity with the customization
specifications in the contract at no additional cost. Otherwise, the purchase shall be
considered irregular that is disallowable in audit.

ManagementComments:
On his letter received by the Office of the Auditor on March 3, 2023, the OIC-SDS
commented that recommendations were duly noted and appropriate measures shall be
utilized to ensure that the implementation of 5D Hub project is in accordance with existing
laws, rules, and regulations. The Management also coordinated with the supplier who
committed to provide the corrected and completed features of the 5D Hub Facility as
required in the contract.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Immediately demand the supplier to replace the items that were not in accordance
with the technical specifications as enumerated in the approved contract; and
b. Require the City General Services Officer (GSO), in the absence of any request for
relief from accountability, to demand in writing the accountable officer of the
production of the missing items. Otherwise, the value of such items will be
recorded as receivable from the concerned official.

We also recommended that the Management strictly comply with the information
required in the contract as provided in COA Circular No. 96-010.

ManagementComments:
Coordination with the supplier by the City GSO on March 30, 2023 disclosed that certain
items were not installed in the agreed specifications to better suit the existing condition of the
cab. Due to very limited space between the roof and ceiling of the cab, two units of aircon
(window type and wall mounted split type) were installed instead of one unit of cassette-type
aircon as indicated in the approved contract. A customized and more expensive Doctortable was provided than the one indicated in the approved contract. Also, two units of sink
were installed separately for convenience and space saving concerns instead of double sink
as indicated in the approved contract. The single bench will be replaced with a single bench
with arm rest in accordance to the approved contract.

AuditorRejoinder:
The recommendation will not be fully complied with until the items replaced are evaluated as
to costing. Submission of proof of replacement of the single bench that is supposed to be
chrome plated and has an arm rest is also recommended.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor:

a. Require the City Treasurer to ensure that additional cash advance is allowed until
and when prior advances are settled; and
b. Designate additional Disbursing Officers to prevent granting of various cash
advances to the same Disbursing Officers.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:

a. Immediately submit the lacking documents noted in Annex H to the Office of the
Auditor for evaluation, otherwise, said transactions shall be suspended; and
b. Strictly comply with Section 4(6) and 39 of PD No. 1445 for future transactions by
ensuring that claims against government funds are supported with complete
documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. AOs to strictly liquidate the cash advances granted to them within the prescribed
timeline pursuant to Section 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
b. Concerned officials/departments to settle beforehand/plan the schedule of
distribution for the payment of wages, honoraria, financial/educational assistance
and other similar payments to avoid incurrence of delay in the liquidation of the
related cash advances.

ManagementComments:
The City Accountant commented that for some cash advances, encashment is only done
when the payroll is scheduled for release to their constituents coming from different
barangays. If the basis of the period to liquidate was the encashment of the check, liquidation
is considered within the prescribed period.

AuditorRejoinder:
If the timeliness of liquidation would be reckoned from the encashment date of checks, this
would then question the premature grant of cash advances. Thus, the Audit Team remains
that in all phases of cash advance, from the grant to liquidation, the Management strictly
abide by the rules set under the COA Circular.

We recommended and the Management agreed to strengthen internal controls on the
use of vehicles by requiring:

a. All drivers to accomplish their daily Driver's Trip Tickets with all the required
information and summarize in chronological order their trips for the month in the
Monthly Report of Official Travels for each vehicle to be submitted to the City GSO;
b. The City General Services Officer to ensure that the fuel loaded does not exceed
the fuel capacity of a particular vehicle; and
c. The City Accountant to verify and ascertain that payments for gasoline, oil and
lubricants are duly supported with completely filled-up driver's trip tickets for
proper evaluation on the propriety and reasonableness of fuel consumption.

ManagementComments:
The City GSO replied that his Office already issued a Memorandum dated February 2, 2023
(attached as 'Annex A' in his letter) to all the official drivers of the City Government to
faithfully and properly accomplish the Driver's Trip Tickets. He will also oblige them to
prepare a Monthly Report of Official Travel in the prescribed format. They will prepare a
logbook per vehicle to monitor the trips undertaken.

The City Accountant also replied that she had already advised the City GSO to strictly comply
with the recommendations and that her office would also strictly adhere with ascertaining that
the duly filled up Driver's Trip Tickets are attached as supporting documents in the payments
for gasoline, oil and lubricants.

We recommended that the City Mayor, thru the City GSO and OIC-CDRRMO,
immediately coordinate with the supplier to put into full operation the City-Wide
Security and Surveillance System. Otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance will be issued
for the technical deficiencies noted in the COA TAS Report.

ManagementComments:
The Management Information System Office of the City Government currently assesses the
status of the system for possible repair to be bid-out from another supplier. The retention
money withheld from the supplier is forfeited to cover any further cost of repair to make the
system operational.

AuditorRejoinder:
The supplier was paid in full despite the unsuccessful rehabilitation of the surveillance system
costing P16,978,847.18, thus not serving its intended purpose. We acknowledged the effort
and desire of the Management to put the City-Wide Security and Surveillace System into full
utilization this 2022 as long as the City Government will not incur additional costs.
Otherwise, payment to the supplier may be considered irregular which is disallowable in
audit.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the:

a. City Accountant, that after the lapse of the warranty period and on the basis of
issuance of the Certification from the City General Services Officer of the delivery
of non-defective goods, process the release of the retention money to the
concerned suppliers; and
b. City Treasurer to keep a copy of the disbursement vouchers where retention
money was withheld to facilitate its release in due time.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Thru the City GSO, ensure that motor vehicles be assigned only to positions
allowed to use government vehicles and those personnel not authorized to use
such be entitled to the common use for the exercise of their core functions;
b. Institute guidelines and internal policies on the use and assignment of vehicles
including its motorpool that would dictate the officials' entitlement to
transportation allowance to ensure the authorized use and facilitate monitoring of
their gasoline consumption; and
c. Discontinue the payment of transportation allowance to City officials with assigned
government motor vehicles in compliance with the applicable GAA, DBM LBC No.
103 dated May 15, 2013 and BC No. 2022-01 dated February 11, 2022.

ManagementComments:
In their reply to the issued Audit Observation Memorandum and during the exit conference,
the Management expressed their intent to strictly comply with the mentioned laws, circulars,
jurisprudence, and other applicable issuances. The given recommendation will be given
particular attention by the City Government which considers adopting a motor pool.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the:

a. OIC - City Health Officer obtain unit cost and quantities of the COVID-19 vaccines
and ancillary supplies; and render a monthly report to the City Accounting Office
on the receipt, issuance and balances of vaccines and other inventories directly
received from outside sources; and
b. City Accountant, to recognize in the books the cost of the ending inventory of
vaccines and supplies as at year-end and to henceforth record future receipts and
issuances of the same in compliance to IPSAS 12 and COA Circular No. 2015-09.

We recommended and the Management agreed that City HRMDO fill-up the vacant
plantilla positions with qualified individuals and ensure that the hiring of JOs is based
on immediate and actual needs of each office/department and with consideration on
the financial capability of the City Government in accordance with Sections 76 and 77
of RA No. 7160 and CSC MC No. 17, series of 2002.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Assessor to evaluate the
appraised values of properties under the jurisdiction of the City based on prevailing
market prices and propose the necessary revisions on the City Code for the year and
every three years thereafter, to comply with the provisions of Sections 201 and 219 of
the Local Government Code of the Philippines.

ManagementComments:
The City Assessor replied on her letter received by the Office of the Auditor on March 31,
2023 that their priority is the massive appraisal and assessment of the properties in the City.
She also added that the conduct of the General Revision in CY 2020 did not push through
because of the COVID-19 pandemic and that they are aware of the period of time set by the
Local Government Code of 1991 but they respectfully submit to take into consideration the
present situation of the people, thus maintains status quo. The present thrust is to collect the
real property tax delinquencies first before the conduct of the General Revision.

AuditorRejoinder:
The Audit Team acknowledged the reply of the City Assessor but reiterated that for the time
being, the City Assessor may already conduct evaluation of the appraised values of
properties under the jurisdiction of the City based on prevailing market prices and propose
the necessary revisions on the City Code.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Veterinarian prepare and
submit periodic inventory report of the animals to the City Accounting Office for the
proper valuation and booking up of the first-born offspring.

We also recommended and the Management agreed that the City Accountant
recognize as Biological Assets the first-born offspring, adjust/revalue accordingly
based on the inventory report of the City Veterinarian, and maintain Biological Asset
Ledger Card for each type of livestock containing the details of the livestock account.

We recommended that the City Mayor and members of the City Development Council
to:

a. Assess the feasibility of the implementation and responsiveness to the current
needs of the constituents of those unimplemented projects; and
b. Ensure optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF by including development
projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready
pursuant to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.

ManagementComments:
The City Engineering Office submitted on March 22, 2023 the status of implementation as of
March 2023 of the 20 percent DF projects which included 55 completed projects or a 54.46
percent completion rate.

The City Planning and Development Officer replied that they have taken note of the
recommendations and will tap the council by including in the Council's Meeting Agenda the
discussion of the observation in order to assess the feasibility of the implementation and
responsiveness to the current need of the constituents of all projects to be implemented.

We recommended and the Management agreed to allocate funds for the next phase
and complete the construction of the buildings to be operational/usable according to
their intended purpose; and

We also recommended and the Management agreed that in cases of construction by
phases for future projects, the City Engineer ensure a clear delineation of work for
each phase up to its completion, which must be usable, and structurally sound
pursuant to Item 4, Annex A of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City DRRM Council:

a. Meticulously plan the programs, projects and activities to be funded out of the
LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities and ensure the viability
thereof; and
b. Maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Funds and to accelerate their
implementation so that the objectives of the funds are fully achieved and the
beneficiaries are protected against natural calamities and emergencies.

1.12 We recommended that the Municipal Assessor:
a) furnish the Municipal Accountant with copies of the Certificates of Title
to facilitate the recording of the titles lots in the LGUs book of accounts;
b) fast-track and supervise the proper proceeding for the land titling of the 37
parcels of land to establish absolute ownership and protect the legal interest
of the Municipality;
c) determine whether the four parcels of land recorded by the Accounting Office
that are still in the name of private individuals are indeed properties of the
Municipality of Tanauan.
1.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor set
specific dates to reconcile their records on the real estate properties owned by the
Municipality.

2.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) direct the Municipal Assessor to prepare and submit to the Municipal
Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first (31st) day of December each year,
an Assessment Roll containing a list of all persons whose real properties have
been newly assessed or reassessed and the values of such properties;
b) require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant of
a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible at the beginning of the year as basis to draw a Journal Entry to record
the RPT and SET Receivables and its corresponding deferred accounts; and
c) direct the Municipal Accountant to examine the history of the accounts of
RPT and SET Receivables and its corresponding deferred accounts to
identify the cause of the discrepancy of ₱2,832,646.21, and prepare
necessary adjusting entries to resolve such discrepancy.

3.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) assess the existing balances in the Due to NGAs account and prepare the
Disbursement Voucher for the return to the Bureau of Treasury of those
identified to be balances from previous fund transfers from the national
government; and
b) revert to the General Fund the unknown amounts, including those in the Due
to LGUs account, and inform the Municipal Budget Officer of such reversion
after confirming with the Municipal Treasurer the availability of the
corresponding cash.

4.7 We recommended that:
a. The Municipal Assessor fast-track the updating of real property
assessments in the Municipality through frequent physical survey and
prepare and submit the Assessment Roll to the Municipal Treasurer; and
b. The Municipal Treasurer conduct intensive tax campaign and
information drive beyond the required posting of notices to encourage
more taxpayers to pay taxes; and follow-up responses to the Notice of
Delinquencies issued.
4.8 We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor impose the legal remedies
and exercise the taxing authority of the LGU as mandated in the Local
Government Code of 1991.

5.9 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the certified list of
uncollected pubic market rentals, and submit the same to the Municipal
Accountant.
5.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the receivables
and the related revenues in the books of the Municipality based on the list from
the Municipal Treasurer.

6.18 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor sign a Lease Contract Agreement
with all existing market stall holders, and enforce the terms and conditions of
the contract in administering the collection of market stall rentals.
6.19 We also recommended that the Market Supervisor and the Municipal
Treasurer maintain a list of all delinquent market stall holders and issue
Demand Letters in accordance with the Lease Contract Agreement Municipal
Treasurer.

7.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the unremitted
balances with the records of the Municipal Treasurer, and make the necessary
adjustments, where necessary, in order to arrive at the correct balance of the
Due to LGU account.

8.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO to include,
under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP, a list of projects and activities to
be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, and to
implement the planned priority projects in a timely manner, in order to
enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the Municipality.

9.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Budget Officer and
the Municipal Accountant discontinue the utilization of the 20% Development
Fund for administrative expenses to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenses.

10.8 We recommended that the Municipality replenish the amount deducted from
the QRF amounting to ₱200,000.00, and to limit the use of the 30% QRF to
activities related to relief and recovery programs of the Municipality.

11.24 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all Department Heads to
indicate in their respective Work and Financial Plans for programs of the
LGU, including the Purchase Request, all the necessary data on goods to be
procured, and to administer properly the planned programs to ensure that the
same are effectively implemented to achieve the desired goals.
11.25 We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee, and the
Secretariat, indicate the complete technical specifications of items to be
procured in all the bidding documents to ensure accurate deliveries.

12.22 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the HRMO to establish a staffing pattern based on the needs of
each department, for approval by appropriate bodies, so that the ideal
number of plantilla personnel will be established, taking into
consideration the Personnel Service limitation;
b. issue written policies and guidelines on the hiring of JOs as guide in
drafting their contracts and in evaluating their accomplishments;
c. limit the hiring of personnel under Job Order/Contract of Service to
those extremely necessary and deploy only to offices that actually are in
need of their services;
d. include in the Job Order/Contract of Service of each JO personnel the
provision on their assigned office or areas, specific duties and
responsibilities, required outputs and period of employment; and
e. discontinue designating JOs and casual personnel as collectors or
liquidating officer and sending JOs to training/seminars.

13.6 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer: (1) deposit the full amount
collected daily to avoid the accumulation of cash in her custody; (2) record
daily in the cashbook all collections and deposits for all funds; and (3) compare
balances per cashbook against actual cash on hand.

14.6 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer maintain a cashbook for
Special Cash Advances and record therein all transactions related to such cash
advance, including the dates of submission of Liquidation Reports to the
Municipal Accountant.

15.23 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. prepare Journal Entry Vouchers to cancel the stale checks and restore
the amount to the Cash in Bank-LCCA and the corresponding liability
account; and adjust the net understatement of ₱36,452.59; and
b. analyze the unsubstantiated reconciling item of ₱184,094.13 under DBP
Account No. 0775-017484-030 in the LGU’s records, and prepare the
adjusting entry to resolve the discrepancy.

16.9 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit to the Bureau of
Treasury the dormant bank accounts to avoid incurring further bank charges and
disallowances.
16.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor prepare a letter to the
Manager of the banks concerned of their intention to close the dormant bank
accounts.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Project Management Team implemented constant and rigid monitoring of infrastructure projects and applied calibrated actions when necessary as prescribed under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019.

We further recommended that, for future receipt of funds from the National Government, the Project Implementation Team implement the purpose of the funds within the period prescribed by the Source Agency in order to prevent the reversion of undisbursed funds.

The Municipal General Service Officer/Head of Inventory Committee :
Conduct an inventory of the local roads under the jurisdiction of the Municipality of Tanauan and prepare a Report on the Physical Count of the Road Networks System, a copy of which must be submitted to the DILG Regional Office;
Prepare a Report on Local Road Network and Road Map;
Maintain and update monthly the Registry of Public Infrastructures  for Roads; and
Maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.
The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Service Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; and
The Municipal Accountant provide complete disclosure of the details of the Road Networks account in the Notes to the Financial Statements, using the format suggested by Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
conduct an inspection of the projects, with the assistance of the Inventory Committee, which are still recorded as on-going under the Construction in-Progress account, to verify its existence/actual condition and prepare a report on the results of such verification to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant; and
prepare a certification of its assessment on the damage of the Tanauan Public Market recorded in the books at ₱37,678,081.95.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Provide the Municipal Engineer with the list of projects still recorded in the books as on-going under the Construction in-Progress account;
Verify, on the basis of the report from the Municipal Engineer, which of the identified projects comprising the ₱5,613,071.04 balance under the Construction in Progress accounts merit proper closure to the appropriate asset accounts and/or restatement; and
Prepare an adjusting entry on the impairment loss of the Tanauan Public Market amounting to ₱37,678,081.95, duly supported with a certificate of the assessment of damage brought about by the Super Typhoon Yolanda from the Municipal Engineer.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee identify and confirm the existence of various PPEs under SEF and submit the list to the Municipal Engineer for appraisal, so that its fair values and remaining useful life can be determined for eventual computation of depreciation expenses for future reporting periods.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the GSO set specific dates to reconcile their records on School Buildings and Other Structures account amounting to ₱984,202.22 and ₱187,458.85, respectively, in order to adjust the asset values of each account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive requiring the Inventory Committee to:
prepare, for his approval, a Physical Inventory Plan containing the assignments of Committee members, schedules/location of inventory-taking and cut-off dates for the complete physical inventory of all properties of the Municipality over a three-month period in CY 2022;
Implement the PIP and finish the physical inventory within the time frame specified in CY 2022.
We further recommended that the Inventory Committee:
consolidate the RPCPPE prepared by each office by grouping together similar items, showing complete data on PPE Type and cost; and
submit the consolidated RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant before December 31, 2022 for reconciliation, copy furnished the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
retrace, with utmost effort, prior years accounting entries affecting the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, in order to resolve the net difference of ₱471,591.36, and effect immediate adjustment in the books; and
present accurately the year-end balances of accounts found in the General Ledger in the year-end financial statements, with the necessary disclosures well-discussed in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Project Management Team:
conduct constant and rigid monitoring of contract completion dates of all infrastructure projects of the Municipality; and
apply promptly, when necessary, the calibrated actions prescribed under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 dated March 8, 2019 on infrastructure projects with perceived negative slippages.

We recommended that in the ensuing years, the Municipal Development Council, through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, identify and rank projects that only effectively meet the requirements of urgency, impact, conflict-compatibility and goal-achievement in the screening process, and those that can substantially be implemented within the specific time schedules during the budget year, in order to strengthen the plan-budget process linkage of the Municipality.
We further recommend that the Municipal Mayor resolve the root causes of unimplemented projects, ensure that selected PPAs are substantially compliant with screening requirements and provide adequate technical personnel to its Project Management Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to return immediately the undisbursed FY 2019 LGSF-AM fund of ₱1,000,000.00 to the Bureau of the Treasury.
We further recommended that, for future receipt of funds from the National Government, the Project Implementation Team implement the purpose of the funds within the period prescribed by the Source Agency in order to prevent the reversion of undisbursed funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the LDRRM Council, through the MDRRM Officer, exclude in the LDRRM Plan in the ensuing years the allocation for stocking of spareparts inventory and comply with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1.
We also recommended that expenses for regular repairs and maintenance of vehicles not directly used for MDRRM activities be charged to the MOOE of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct a physical count of the items that remain in the Stock Room of the Supply Office and prepare the corresponding report for submission to the Municipal Accountant, supported with a sworn certification of the Supply Officer that all inventories procured in prior years were fully issued to the intended end-users.

We also recommended that the Supply Officer prepare a pre-numbered Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) for every issuance/release of supplies and inventories to end-users and summarize monthly said RIS in the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) for submission to the Municipal Accountant within five days after the end of the month.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reflect the correct balance of the Inventory account after reconciliation with the report of the Inventory Committee.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council (MDC), through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer:

Update the current year Annual Investment Plan to indicate the specific projects, location and other necessary details for each PPA to be funded from current year and continuing appropriations of the 20% Development Fund in order to avoid seeking additional authority from the SB when projects are implemented; and

Review, during the preparation of the annual budget, the necessity and viability of the PPAs under the Continuing Appropriations of the 20% Development Fund and re-appropriate the unused amounts for other development projects, if the previous PPAs are no longer needed.

We recommended that the LDRRM Focal Person prepare the Supplemental LDRRMFIP for CY 2020 that will contain the Covid-19 related expenditures paid from the Special Trust Fund and submit to the LDRRM Council Resolution for approval of the said disbursements.  Henceforth, include at all times in the annual LDRRMFIP under a separate caption, the projects, programs and activities chargeable from the prior years’ unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council, through the LDRRM Focal Person, update the amount allocated for each PPA to be funded from the Continuing Appropriations of LDRRMF and implement the same during the budget year, otherwise, substitute it with other PPAs which are needed, implementable and can improve the disaster response and rescue capability of the Municipality.

We recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat comply with the mandatory posting and reporting requirement in the GPPB online portal of all POs/Contracts relative to the Emergency Procurement in response to Covid-19 pandemic as mandated in Section 3.6 of GPPB Circular No. 1-2020 dated April 6, 2020, and GPPB – TSO Advisory 05-2020, dated June 17, 2020.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System of the Municipality:

Include only specific gender issues (with reference to women or girl children) / GAD mandates to be addressed and gender responsive programs, projects and activities in the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that management prioritize and provide adequate funding for the construction of the Sanitary Landfill pursuant to Section 37 of Republic Act No. 9003 to hasten the closure of the open dumpsite and to ensure the protection of public health.

We recommend that the municipality maximize utilization of appropriated funds in implementing the various programs for risk reduction and management in order to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities.  Likewise, 
require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to transfer to the Special Trust Fund the unexpended balances of LDRRMF for CY 2016 and CY 2017 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant record the Inventories Held for Distribution and for Consumption according to the correct quantity on hand and its valuation as of year-end supported with inventory reports and supplies ledger cards.
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant adjust Due to BIR to the actual amounts of taxes withheld as of December 31, 2016 totaling P775,051.76 and henceforth monitor taxes withheld and remitted.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor should require officials concerned to strictly observe Section 20 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. I. He should direct the Municipal treasurer to prioritize the updating of the RPTARs and come up with the certified list of taxpayers showing the realty taxes due so that the Municipal Accountant shall have a correct basis in the setting up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to: a) provide the City Accountant and the City GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of the road projects; b) City Engineer to complete the data for road projects, and c) City Accountant to ensure proper disclosure in Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office coordinated with the Accounting Office for provision of data. The City Accountant confirmed coordination and continuing work to incorporate road project data.
observations.reason: The related data for road projects were not yet provided by the City Engineering Office to the City Accountant at time of report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor coordinate with the Catholic Bishop of Lipa for possible donation/purchase of the subject land where the school buildings lie; and the City to provide complete documentation and secure legal title for lands.
observations.managementAction: The City made representations and coordinated for possible donation/purchase with the Catholic Bishop of Lipa and requested copies/letters showing status. The City provided communication and is coordinating.
observations.reason: The Team was not furnished a copy of the requested representation letter or documentation showing the City's legal basis for inclusion of the land; the matter remains pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City request for write-off of receivables and file with the Commission on Audit in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 after exhausting all available remedies.
observations.managementAction: No action taken yet. Management continues to exhaust all possible means to collect outstanding receivables before requesting write-off.
observations.reason: Submission of all proofs that efforts have been initiated to collect the receivables should be undertaken prior to filing for write-off. Management is still exhausting all possible remedies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer/Cashier to deposit all collections intact daily or on the next banking day; that the City Treasurer to ensure DSCAF are properly signed and that City Accountant to require TRAAF with RAAF attachments be submitted to COA by the 5th day of the ensuing month.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer and Accounting Office reported procedural improvements, including transmittal reporting and ensuring daily deposits. However, some lapses continue when volume is high.
observations.reason: Instances of deviation from procedures persisted; some deposits were made by liquidating officers due to operational constraints. Enforcement ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and City Accountant with the help of the City Cooperative Office to: • issue demand letters to TPMMPC member-borrowers for the collection of their outstanding balance and immediately remit the collections to the Source Agency; and • write-off the amount if collection is not feasible after exhausting remedies and file write-off with COA.
observations.managementAction: No action taken. The City did not issue demand letters; management submitted request to write-off and is seeking Presidential Management assistance.
observations.reason: No demand letters were issued; instead, formal request to Presidential Management forwarded for write-off of amount P366,667.67. Collection efforts not successful.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to submit
immediately the 845 disbursement vouchers including the supporting documents to
the Audit Team to facilitate post-audit, otherwise a Notice of Suspension shall be
issued, pursuant to the 2009 RRSA prescribed under COA Circular 2009-006. We also
recommended strict compliance with the provisions of Section 347 of RA No. 7160,
Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987, COA Circular No. 2009-006, and
Sections 39 and 100 of PD No. 1445 on prompt submission of accounts, otherwise
enforce the provision of Section 122 of PD No. 1445 against concerned officials.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Accountant to: (a) submit
immediately the liquidation reports of CF of P2,703,000.00 to COA Intelligence and
Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU), and record the liquidation supported with duly
acknowledged transmittal letter; (b) ensure strict compliance with Items 6.2.2 and
6.2.2.7 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND JC No. 2015-01 on the time/period of liquidation
of cash advance; and (c) record the succeeding advances for CF to Advances to
Officers and Employees.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant to:
 Review the entries in the Due From Other Funds in the General Fund viz-a-viz
entries in the Due to General Fund in the SEF and Trust Fund, and effect the
necessary adjustment/s in the appropriate books; and
 Henceforth, ensure that Due to/from Other Funds are reconciled every accounting
period.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to require the City Accountant to draw a
JEV to reverse the entry made under JEV No, 2020-09-50153 dated September 30,
2020.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to enjoin the City Accountant to:
a. Submit the DV for the grant of cash advance for CF together with all the required
and proper supporting documents within the prescribed period in a sealed
envelope with a visible label "CONFIDENTIAL- For the COA ATL Only" with a
covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents; and
b. Ensure that the grant of cash advance for CF are complete with supporting
documents, and submit the following lacking documentary requirements for the
amount of P6,645,000.00:
 Certified copy of the minutes of the meeting of the 2/3 votes of the City Peace
and Order Council (CPOC) approving the Peace and Order Program (POP) and
the release of CF; and
 Certification from the Chief of Police highlighting the peace and order situation
in the locality and supporting the need to release and use the CF.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
 Create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee that will facilitate the appraisal,
inspection and disposal of the unserviceable property;
 Instruct the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to accomplish the required IIRUP
and submit to the COA Audit Team together with the required documents for
review and evaluation; and
 Cause the immediate disposal of the unserviceable property thru public auction or
negotiated sale for justifiable reasons.

We recommended that the Management implement Sections 170 and 171 of RA No.
7160 wherein the function of assessment of business taxes and regulatory fees shall
be performed by the City Treasurer.

We further recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod review and amend the
Local Revenue Code particularly Item ii of Section 3A.04 to realign its provisions with
RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct her Chief of Staff and CDRRMO to:
 Prepare plans/programs with specific purpose for the full implementation of the
received donations, fast track its utilization and ensure that these are utilized for
the intended purpose in a timely manner;
 Submit to the Office of the Auditor copy of the duly accomplished and signed
MOA and Acknowledgement of Calamity Assistance from PCSO with Undertaking
to Comply; and
 Strictly adhere to the documentary and liquidation requirements of the calamity
assistance and the stipulated conditions agreed upon in the Memorandum of
Agreement to assure proper utilization and accountability over the funds.

We also recommended that the City Accountant record and include the received
funds in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).

We recommended the City to create a special account in the general fund (SAGF)
for the Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities (BGCM) through an ordinance
by the local sanggunian, for easy monitoring/tracking of the utilization of the
grant/fund, pursuant to Section 3.7 of DBM LBC No. 125.

Also, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) to ensure that all procurement projects of the City regardless of modality are
properly included in the approved APP and all changes thereto are covered by
supplemental/revised APP. Also, submit satisfactory explanation for the procurement
of P49,403,197.35 not covered with approved supplemental APP.

Moreover, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. BAC Secretariat to comply with the posting requirements of GPPB Circular
No. 2020-001 for information purposes and transparency;
b. City Accountant to prepare a monthly report on fund utilization and status of
implementation of PPAs under the Bayanihan Grant, in compliance with the
provisions on Item 4 of DBM LBC No. 125.
We also recommended that the City Mayor require the Management and Information
System Office to update the City’s website and make the necessary posting as part of
their public disclosure requirements.

Further, we recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the
BAC to submit the: a) lacking documents presented in Appendix J to the Audit
Team for verification; and b) justification/explanation to address the other
deficiencies enumerated.

Lastly, we recommended to enjoin the City Accountant to ensure that all documentary
requirements for emergency purchase under RA No. 11469 are complied with before
certifying as to the completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement
vouchers.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the:
 City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) and the City Treasurer’s
Office to: (a) submit a written satisfactory explanation/justification for the
distribution of SAP-ESP beyond the prescribed period and non- issuance of OR
on the returned SAP-ESP; and (b) account the returned SAP-ESP;
 CSWDO to inform the 271 ineligible beneficiaries to return the financial
assistance received, otherwise a Notice a Disallowance shall be issued; and
 CSWDO to submit the lacking documentary requirements listed in Appendix M
and provide justification for the deficient documents to avoid issuance of Notice
of Suspension. Moreover, review the documentary papers of beneficiaries for
transactions not yet included in the Audit Team’s audit/sample, and require the
submission of lacking documents, if there are any.

We recommended that the City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) to
submit:
 the lacking documentary papers listed in Appendix N and a written satisfactory
explanation/justification for the lapses/deficiencies noted on the supporting
documents submitted; and
 report/s to document the: a) replacement of LSAP beneficiaries; and b) return of
the LSAP from paid beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CSWD Office to submit a written
satisfactory justification on the variances on the number of households provided
with assistance against the number of households assessed and recommended by
their office, and number of households per masterlist attached to the payroll.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to revert the
unutilized balance of DRRM funds amounting to P16,337,743.01 to the Unappropriated
Surplus of the General Fund so that it shall be made available for social services after
subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City’s Gender and Development
Focal Person to prepare the Annual Gender and Development Plan and Budget within
the time frame set and submit on or before March 31 to the PPDO and DILG Provincial
Office for review and endorsement, in accordance with PCW DILG DBM and NEDA
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the: A. City Accountant and OIC – City General Services Officer to: a) Reconcile the Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts and effect the necessary adjustments in the appropriate records; b) Exert extra effort in tracing and locating the pertinent records/documents for the proper recording in the RPCPPE and books of accounts; c) Effect, in respective records, the necessary adjustments on the misclassified 34 PPE, per Appendix E.3, to their proper PPE classification in accordance with the revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units. B. City Accountant to compute/adjust the depreciation expenses on unrecorded and misclassified PPE and effect the necessary adjustment in the books through Journal Entry Voucher.
observations.managementAction: A. City Accountant to: a) Reconcile the Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts and effect the necessary adjustments in the appropriate records; b) Exert extra effort in tracing and locating the pertinent records/documents for the proper recording in the RPCPPE and books of accounts; c) Effect, in respective records, the necessary adjustments on the misclassified 34 PPE, per Appendix E.3, to their proper PPE classification in accordance with the revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units. B. City Accountant to compute/adjust the depreciation expenses on unrecorded and misclassified PPE and effect the necessary adjustment in the books through Journal Entry Voucher.
observations.reason: The Infrastructure and Buildings and Other Structures accounts were reconciled. JEV Nos. 100-2020-08-399 and 2020-08-401 dated July 08, 2020 were made to reclassify to their proper PPE accounts and to adjust the corresponding depreciation expenses of the 34 PPE items. The depreciation expenses of the 34 misclassified PPE items were already adjusted. The depreciation expenses of the 27 unrecorded/unappraised PPE items were not yet adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to coordinate with the barangays in the implementation of Real Property Tax Program; and strictly implement the remedies and sanctions provided in RA No. 7160 to deter delay and non-payment of taxes.
observations.managementAction: The management is already undertaking coordination with the 48 barangays in the locality as well as house-to-house campaign, campus tour and pitching in with various organizations to explain the bases and the rationale in paying the RPT.
observations.reason: Taal Volcano eruption and COVID-19 pandemic affected the full implementation of the agency’s action plan for collection.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Office (GSO) to: • stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions and POs/contracts pursuant to COA Circular No. 76-41 Section 54.1 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184, to promote competition and transparency in the procurement process; • consolidate the goods/supplies and procure in bulk on a monthly or quarterly basis in accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan and as provided under the RIRR of RA No. 9184; and • provide justification why the said transactions totaling P5,957,796.16 should not be considered irregular and disallowable in audit.
observations.managementAction: In a letter dated August 5, 2020 signed by Acting GSO and noted by the City Administrator, the management averred that only few suppliers are interested/willing to supply the items being requested/ canvassed, while other suppliers refused to participate, for fear of rejection by the new administration.
observations.reason: Recording of various office supplies, hardware and spare parts procurement of supplies was not implemented because of the varied nature of request, dates of use and activities to be performed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor adhere to the provisions of the IRR of RA No. 9433 by requiring the Human Resources Officer and the City Social Development Officer of identifying and determining specific positions that are to eligible to receive Hazard Pay are requested to submit Daily Time Record and accomplishment report prior to determine their specific level of exposure to occupational risks.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer commented that effective June 2020, employees eligible for hazard pay are requested to submit Daily Time Record and accomplishment report to determine their level of exposure to hazards and risks. The City planned to assess which positions are eligible.
observations.reason: There were instances that some documents were not submitted; therefore a Notice of Suspension will be issued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Human Resource Management and Development Officer to: • submit the duties and responsibilities of the hired EAs together with their contract which was only required by Memorandum No. HR-2020-08-07-147 dated August 7, 2020 issued by the OIC-HRMDO and noted by the City Administrator; • regularly furnish COA copies of the Job Order contract to facilitate the timely review and inspection; and • adhere with the Civil Service Commission’s and other governing rules in hiring Job Order employees.
observations.managementAction: The management was able to submit on August 17, 2020 the duties and responsibitites of the hired EAs. The City assessed its staffing needs to determine appropriate manpower complement for their programs/activities/projects and to determine compliance with CSC, COA and DBM rules. The City is conducting assessment. The City is able to submit copies of Job Order contract. The City is assessing manpower and continues compliance.
observations.reason: The draft for the hired EAs was not yet submitted to the Office of the Auditor. Submission of memorandum no. was required starting August 07, 2020 as implemented by the City Mayor. The City is still completing documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the: a) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit copies of contracts/Purchase Orders together with their supporting documents, Request for Inspection, Notice of Delivery, and Acceptance and Inspection Reports of Delivery and Acceptance to COA within five working days after receipt/acceptance of goods, for the timely review and inspection; and b) enjoin the Procuring Officer to always see to it that the POs contain, among others, information as to the date of delivery, duly signed by the supplier and the City Mayor; and provide complete information of POs including Delivery and Payment Terms as discussed in Observation no. 9.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer commented that matters regarding the inclusion of unnecessary work items in the detailed engineering estimates were explained and justified. The City is coordinating and is making adjustments to ensure completeness of PO documentation.
observations.reason: Some POs were still not submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period. Some POs lacked date signed by supplier and City Mayor; some lacked Delivery and Payment Terms; thus still implementation pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to coordinate with the concerned suppliers with unreleased retention money for their claim/s and in accordance with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office identified and analyzed the unclaimed retention money. The amount was reverted in CY 2020 from the Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable account. The amount reverted was P3,670,882.03 (General Fund) and P178,867.38 (SEF).

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to countersign all the checks drawn by the City Treasurer pursuant to Section 345 of RA No. 7160 and Section 43 of COA Circular No. 92-382.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer transmitted a request to the Sangguniang Panglungsod Committee on Laws and Privileges on July 24, 2020 requesting authority to sign checks. The Sangguniang Panglungsod considered the matter.
observations.reason: Delay in the submission of the request for authority to sign checks to the Sangguniang Panlungsod; action pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Accountant in coordination with the City Treasurer to determine the cause of delay in the processing of documents to facilitate timely payments to the contractors pursuant to Item 40.3 Section General Conditions of Contract.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer commented on his letter dated November 29, 2019 that the delay in processing the documents was due to incomplete documentation. The City is implementing improved documentation procedures.
observations.reason: There were still pending documents; the reason for delay was incomplete documentation. Some payments were processed once documents were complete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: • establish or formulate policies and guidelines in the granting of donation in kind to its Barangays, NGOs/POs, schools and other associations/organizations and require proof of actual utilization of the materials for the intended programs/projects/activities within a reasonable time after receipt of the materials; • the GSO to consolidate Purchase Requests to be purchased and procure them in bulk with the approved Annual Procurement Program and avoid piece-meal procurements; • provide justification letter on why the materials/items totaling P12,964,034.71 were procured on piece-meal basis; and • provide justification/explanation why donations extended to private entities/organizations amounting to P426,773.81 should not be considered irregular and disallowable in audit.
observations.managementAction: The City OIC-GSO explained that piece-meal procurement of supplies was not done due to the varied nature of request, dates of use and activities to be performed. The City is to comply with requirements and provide justifications as necessary. The City stopped practice of giving donations in kind with pictures/names of officials. For the cash donations, City notified barangays to prepare liquidation reports and submit to accounting. Regarding donations to private entities, no justification was submitted to the Audit Team; request for issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
observations.reason: Some actions were taken: reverted amounts to prior period adjustments; donation procedures adjusted; but documentation and MOAs were not consistently provided and some procurements remained piece-meal. Some donations lacked supporting MOAs and justifications; issuance of Notice of Disallowance pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to: a) review the procedures in the implementation of infrastructure projects by administration and comply with the rules and regulations on project implementation by administration; b) submit the Status of Work Accomplished and As-Built Plans for the projects implemented; c) submit contract between City Government and Job Orders; d) provide explanations/justifications for delays in payment of wages, gaps, erasures in DTRs and the propriety of 14 days work in specific installations; and e) City Sports Development Officer to require refund of amount P9,000.00 representing gas payment. Also require BAC Secretariat to submit documents of JJ Transport for procurement compliance; and City Accountant to check completeness.
observations.managementAction: The City replied that the documentation was submitted to support payments where available; the BAC and project engineers provided explanations; some supporting documents were submitted to COA; for several items, documentation was lacking and the Audit Team will issue Notice of Suspension where necessary.
observations.reason: No refund was made as of writing; some documents were submitted but others missing; the Audit Team to issue Notice of Suspension for unresolved items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: • Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit justification letter on why they allowed the inclusion of unnecessary items; • Project Engineers to submit explanation on the differences of the installed CCTV cameras as against the approved program of work and specifications; and • Assets Builders Corporation to refund the amount of P2,040,389.29 based on COA-TSO Evaluation Report.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer commented that the deviation from the specifications of the installed CCTV cameras was due to differences in site conditions and that the contractor adapted the installation to actual site requirements without additional cost.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to: a) review the procedures in the implementation of the Pakyaw Labor Contracting System; b) submit Status of Work Accomplished and As-Built Plans; c) submit contract entered into with job orders; d) provide explanations for delays in payment of wages, gaps from receipt of materials to implementation; and e) ensure completeness and propriety of supporting documents before effecting payments.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer replied that the procurement of infrastructure projects under administration would be supported by required attachments; Status of Work and As-Built plans were being prepared and attachments were being submitted to support payments.
observations.reason: The status of work and as-built plans were not yet submitted to the Audit Team at time of report; attachments are being prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a) City Treasurer and the City Accountant to look into the root cause of delayed preparation and submission of the financial reports and disbursement vouchers and take necessary actions to ensure adequate measures; b) City Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed timeframe for submission of monthly and quarterly reports and prepare transmittal letters with supporting documents; and c) City Accountant to coordinate with the City Treasurer for the latter to submit immediately the required reports within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer and City Accountant indicated coordination between the Treasury and Accounting Offices has been improved; City Accounting Office instructed staff to prepare transmittal reports for each fund; however, due to voluminous transactions and staff constraints, delays were noted.
observations.reason: Delays in submission of financial reports and disbursement vouchers continued due to voluminous transactions and staff limitations; improvements ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to: • compel the TRAAF Accountable Officers to prepare a correct and reliable TRAAF and to submit the same together with the RAAF of Collectors to the Office of the Audit Team Leader not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month; • require all accountable collectors to submit their individual Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms at the end of the month as basis for the preparation and submission of the CRAAF.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer indicated that copies of TRAAF including attached RAAFs were being submitted to the Audit Team and that the accounting office requires such documents as attachment to payroll.
observations.reason: Some RAAFs and supporting documents were submitted; reconciliation ongoing. Some collectors still failed to timely submit the required reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor encourage and support the establishment of Records Management including filing, safekeeping/preservation and disposal of government records including vouchers and files, thus creating a system of internal control and that City files be kept for immediate reference.
observations.managementAction: The City maintained that the City files are being coordinated and that procedures are being observed per COA circulars. The City reminded staff to follow record management procedures.
observations.reason: Some records were still being held by staff; the City is working to centralize records and ensure immediate reference availability.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor maximize the utilization of the 70 percent LDRRMF to enable the City to implement projects related to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness. We also recommended that the CDRRMC, with the help of the City Planning and Development Office and City Engineering Office, review and update the LDRRMF unexpended balance and incorporate PPAs to be funded by the unexpended QRF and MOOE of previous years so that the City will be fully equipped and can effectively respond during calamities.
observations.managementAction: The City CDRRMO, Budget Office and Accounting Office coordinated and reconciled records. The total obligations for the 70 per cent mitigation fund in CY 2020 amounted to P27,484,460.54.
observations.reason: Monitoring and evaluation of the LDRRMF implementation is being undertaken; reconciliation of records was performed. The utilization rate improved but remained below target; implementation affected by COVID-19 and 2020 priorities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the CDRRMC to: • decide on the use of the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF, review and update annually the LDRRMF unexpended balance and list the PPAs to be funded by the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years; and • accomplish the LDRRMFIP by providing sufficient information in the listed PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMO submitted proposed PPAs to the LCE for consideration. City issued a resolution authorizing use of part of the funds for infrastructure projects. The LDRRMFIP was completed.
observations.reason: The LDRRMFIP was completed and PPAs incorporated. Some amounts remained pending action and required approval by the Sanggunian; parts reverted as authorized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Development Council, City Planning and Development Office, City Engineering Office and other offices concerned to improve planning, scheduling of work activities and manpower in order to facilitate the timely completion of the projects and to strive in improving the implementation of projects under the 20 per cent Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: The management stated that the City implemented 22 projects under continuing appropriations of P36,307,631.66, eight of which were from CY 2019. The City allocated P90,276,950.00 for COVID-19 related expenses under the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Full implementation of the 20% Development Fund did not materialize due to COVID-19 pandemic; utilization rate for CY 2020 was 67 percent.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and all Accountable Officers to: • stop immediately the wrongful and irregular practice of delayed and partial deposits of collections; • deposit all collections intact daily or not later than the next banking day to avoid the risk of loss or misuse of money; and • ensure all cash collections are deposited with the Authorized Government Depository Bank.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer stated that collections are being faithfully remitted to LBP and procedures instituted to deposit funds daily; however, there were instances of delay due to understaffing and large volumes.
observations.reason: Some collections were still retained in custody pending deposits; staffing constraints and volume caused delays. Procedures improved but not fully complied with at all times.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the CSWDO and City Treasurer to: • complete the required information in the payrolls by submitting copies of OSCA IDs with signature or fingerprint of the beneficiaries; • attach signatures or fingerprint to validate payrolls; and • account for unrefunded unclaimed stipends amounting to P75,000.00.
observations.managementAction: The CSWD provided copies of OSCA IDs for some beneficiaries; they created a team to retrieve and complete lacking documents and coordinated with barangays. On non-submission of ORs and returns, they documented returns with certifications and photographs.
observations.reason: Only some OSCA IDs were submitted; 45 beneficiaries lacked signatures/fingerprints; CSWD continues to retrieve missing documents. Returned stipends documented but ORs not properly issued for returns; some documentation pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Administrator, City Accountant and TCGEU President: • submit the internal guidelines and specific authority/legal basis for granting the CNA incentive and the specific criteria for determining entitlements and distribution; • identify cost-cutting measures and system improvement that management and union should undertake; and • provide complete supporting documents as required by Budget Circular to avoid audit suspensions and disallowances.
observations.managementAction: The City provided attachments to payrolls and indicated that internal criteria and ordinance basis were being used; implementing guidelines being attached to payroll.
observations.reason: The City provided some supporting documents and identified cost-saving measures. Complete documentation and formal internal guidelines still being compiled and attached to payrolls.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to: • return the unissued and defective AFs/ORs to NPO for replacement; • ensure that a sound system of internal control is in place; and • observe procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2013-006 in request for documents/records and to request originals where needed.
observations.managementAction: The City indicated that unissued defective AFs/ORs were being inspected and returned to NPO; flap initials required; ticket checkers instructed to indicate receipt and initials. Some defective AFs were not returned as they were purchased years ago.
observations.reason: A thorough inspection of AFs/ORs upon receipt from NPO is being conducted. Some defective AFs were not returned to NPO because they were purchased years ago; some missing accountability forms still under investigation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer compel the Accountable Officers to prepare a correct and reliable TRAAF together with RAAF of Collectors and submit the same to the COA not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month and ensure accuracy in reporting serial numbers and cancellation.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer reported that copies of TRAAF including attached RAAFs of accountable officers were being submitted to the Audit Team. The Accounting Office requires attachments to payroll.
observations.reason: Some corrections made; reconciliation ongoing. Some accountable forms still not reported; discrepancies remain being reconciled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor encourage the promotion of cooperatives or associations to help implement RA No. 9003; initiate the establishment of Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) for the 48 barangays of the City; and provide adequate appropriation for MRF for the 48 barangays.
observations.managementAction: The City reported that studies were being conducted on possibility of establishing cooperatives and MRF; coordination with NGOs was being pursued. Some projects were pending due to the Taal Volcano eruption and COVID-19 pandemic.
observations.reason: Due to Taal Volcano eruption and COVID-19 pandemic, establishment of MRF and promotion of cooperatives was not pushed through; some actions in progress.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust Due to BIR to the actual amounts of taxes withheld as of December 31, 2016 totaling P775,051.76 and henceforth monitor taxes withheld and remitted.
observations.reason: Part of the taxes withheld do not have records on file, hence could not be fully remitted to BIR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to work closely to account for the difference of P407,431.75; and make the necessary reclassifying journal entries for the items which should not be validly classified under the account duly supported with the appropriate supporting documents, and adjust the Cash in Vault (101) account to its correct balance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant has yet to reconcile the difference. The Municipal Accountant cannot reclassify these items without proper documents or legal basis.
observations.reason: Municipal Treasurer and Accountant has yet to reconcile the difference. Items enumerated in the findings should not be included in the CIV were not yet classified. According to the Municipal Accountant, the concerned items existed prior to NGAS and the Accountant cannot reclassify these items without proper documents or legal basis.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the:

A. City Accountant and OIC – City General Services Officer to:
a) Reconcile the Infrastructure Assets, and Buildings and Other Structures
accounts and effect the necessary adjustments in the appropriate records;
b) Exert extra effort in tracing and locating the pertinent records/documents/
information for the cost of 27 PPE for proper recording in the RPCPPE and
books of accounts; and
c) Effect, in respective records, the necessary adjustments on the
misclassified 34 PPE, per Appendix E.3, to their proper PPE account
classification in accordance with the revised Chart of Accounts for Local
Government Units; and
B. City Accountant to compute/adjust the depreciation expenses on unrecorded
and misclassified PPE and effect the necessary adjustment in the books
through Journal Entry Voucher.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to coordinate with
the barangays in the implementation of Real Property Tax Program; and strictly
implement the remedies and sanctions provided in RA No. 7160 to deter delay and
non-payment of taxes.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Officer (GSO)
to:
• stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions and POs/contracts
pursuant to COA Circular No. 76-41 and Section 54.1 of the RIRR of RA No.
9184, to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement
process;
• consolidate the needed goods/supplies and procure in bulk on a monthly or
quarterly basis in accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan and
as provided under the RIRR of RA No. 9184; and
• provide justification why the said transactions totaling P5,957,796.16 should
not be considered irregular and disallowable in audit.

We recommended that the City Government strictly adhere to the provisions of
the IRR of RA No. 9433 by requiring the Human Resources Officer and City Social
Development Officer of identifying and determining specific positions that are
eligible to receive hazard pay with descriptions as to actual exposure to
occupational risks including the duration of such exposure and assignment to
places mentioned in Section 16.1.1 of the IRR, duly approved by the Honorable
Mayor.

We further recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Social Workers
mentioned in Annex G to submit proof of actual exposure to occupational risk and
duration of their exposure to substantiate their money claims of Hazard Pay
equivalent to 20 percent of their basic pay.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Human Resource Management
and Development Officer to:
• submit the duties and responsibilities of the hired EAs under Job Order
contract together with their accomplishment reports;
• regularly furnish COA copies of the Job Order contract services to facilitate
timely review and evaluation of the transactions; and
• adhere with the Civil Service Commission’s and other governing rules in hiring
Job Order employees.

We further recommended that the City replace the four Job Orders employees by
designating a permanent/regular employees as market collectors to protect the
interest of the government in case of loss thru theft or pilferages.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City’s General Services and
