Engineering Office to:

a. Submit copies of contracts/Purchase Orders, together with their supporting
documents, and Request for Inspection, Notice of Delivery and Acceptance
and Inspection Reports to COA within five working days after their
perfection and within 24 hours from receipt/acceptance of goods,
respectively, for the timely review and inspection; and
b. Enjoin the Procuring Officer to always see to it that the POs contain, among
others, information as to the date of delivery, conformed date of supplier
and procurement terms for easy determination of the full compliance with
the terms and conditions of the contract.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to coordinate
with the concerned suppliers with unreleased retention money for the necessary
action/s to be undertaken by the latter to facilitate their claim/s and in accordance
with Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We reiterated that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to countersign all
the checks drawn by the City Treasurer pursuant to Section 345 of RA No. 7160
and Section 43 of COA Circular No. 92-382.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Accountant in coordination
with the City Treasurer to determine the cause of delay in the processing of
documents to facilitate timely payments to the contractors pursuant to Item 40.3
Section IV General Conditions of Contract of the Philippine Bidding Documents.

We recommended that Management establish or formulate specific guidelines and
policies in the granting of financial assistance in kind to its component
Barangays, Non-Government Organizations/People’s Organizations (NGOs/POs),
schools and other associations/organizations and require a proof of actual
utilization of the materials for the intended programs/projects/activities within a
reasonable time after receipt of the materials.

b) Donated items totaling P12,964,034.71 were procured on a piece-meal basis,
thru Negotiated Procurement instead of Public Bidding as required in Section
10 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, hence defeating the policy of the
law to afford greater transparency and competitiveness in government
procurement and to obtain the most advantageous terms and conditions for
government.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the:

a. General Services Office to consolidate all the Purchase Requests and
items/materials to be purchased and procure them in bulk in
accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Program to avoid
splitting of purchases.
b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to recommend appropriate
method of procurement and encourage competitive bidding instead of
alternative modes, when the circumstances allow, and avoid splitting
of purchases to promote effective competition and transparency in the
procurement process and to obtain a fair and equitable contract for
the best interest of the government otherwise, piece-meal
procurements in the future will be suspended/disallowed in audit.
c. General Services Office to submit justification letter on why the
materials/items totaling P12,964,034.71 were splitted/procured on
piece-meal basis, otherwise these will be considered irregular and
disallowable in audit.

Likewise, post purchases with an Approved Budget for the Contract
exceeding P50,000.00 in the PhilGEPS portal and ensure that the use of
alternative mode of procurement is supported by BAC Resolution
recommending and justifying to the Head of the Procuring Entity (HOPE)
the use of alternative mode of procurement.

We also recommended that the City Accountant check the completeness
and propriety of the supporting documents before disbursing funds.

tc) Donations amounting to P426,773.81 bore the pictures and names of various
City Elected Officials, contrary to Department of the Interior and Local
Government (DILG) Memorandum Circular (MC) No. 2010-101 and Sections
2.2.6 to 2.2.8 of Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2013-004 on
Information and Publicity on Programs/Projects/Activities of Government
Agencies.

We recommended that the City Government of Tanauan:
• Provide justification/explanation letter why the transactions totaling
P426,773.81 should not be considered unnecessary and disallowable in
audit.
• Comply with Sections 2.2.6 to 2.2.8 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 and DILG
MC No. 2010-101 and refrain from placing pictures and/or names of City
Elected Officials on government programs, projects and properties.

d) Donations to barangays amounting to P12,775,901.85 were not recorded in the
latter’s’ books of accounts indicating weak internal control on the part of both
Local Government Units (LGUs) in securing the accountability for these
donations and may result in loss of government resources due to
misuse/misapplication.

We recommended that the City Accountant direct the Barangay Record
keepers/Treasurers to record in their respective reports all grants and
donations in kind received from the City Government:

Suggested Journal Entries for:
• Inventoriable items amounting to P2,305,595.51: (Dr) Other Supplies and
Materials Inventory, (Cr) Grants and Donations in Kind; and
• PPE items amounting to P10,470,306.34: (Dr) Furniture and Fixtures,
(Cr) Grants and Donations in Kind.

e) Donations totaling P102,372.57 were given to various private associations/
organizations/school, in violation of Section 335 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160,
thus detrimental to the interest of the City government.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
• Provide justification letter on why the donation in kind extended to private
entities/organization totaling P102,372.57 will not be considered irregular and
disallowable in audit.
• Stop the practice of extending financial assistance for purposes which are
private in character in conformity with the provisions of Section 335 of RA
No. 7160, otherwise it will be disallowed in audit.

• Donations in cash – P5,734,912.00

a) Financial Assistance to Barangays and Non-Government Organizations/
People’s Organizations (NGOs/POs) totaling P5,726,912.00 were not
supported with a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and other supporting
documents thereby failing to establish the responsibility and accountability
of the Barangays and NGOs/POs in the disposition and utilization of funds,
and casting doubt on the propriety of the fund transfers made.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Legal Officer that all
transfer of funds to Barangays be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement
(MOA) and instruct the City Accountant to require submission of other
documentary requirements as enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular
No. 2012-001 prior to the release of financial assistance, cash or in kind, to
establish proper accountability, and to ensure that the public funds were
actually spent for the purposes they were requested.

We recommended also that the projects proposed to be funded out or to be
charged to the assistance funds be regularly monitored by the donor City
thru its monitoring team to ensure efficient and effective utilization of the
funds and/or to prevent misuse or misapplication of the same.

b) Financial assistance granted to a private group/association amounting to
P8,000.00 was contrary to Section 335 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor provide justification/explanation
letter why the said transactions amounting to P8,000.00 should not be
considered irregular and disallowable in audit.

We further recommended that management be guided by Section 335 of
RA No. 7160. Grants or disbursements that are contrary or not allowed
pursuant to laws, rules and regulations should be discontinued otherwise
it will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a) City Accountant to execute the certification that previous cash advances have
been liquidated and accounted for in the books or the person drawing cash
advance has no outstanding/unliquidated cash advance, and attached it to the
disbursement vouchers before the grant of cash advance.
b) City Sports Development Officer to instruct his personnel to submit the
following:
b.1) Itinerary of Travel, Certificate of Travel Completed and Certificate of
Appearance for the plane fare and bus tickets;
b.2) The list of bus with the corresponding plate numbers as well as copy of
the LTO registrations;
b.3) The names of the drivers who operated those vehicles and copy of their
updated driver’s license;
b.4) List/names of passengers or passengers manifest for each bus;
b.5) Official Receipt for the payment of food and other supplies amounting to
P33,613.79 as per Appendix L.2;
b.6) Certification from officials/employees for expenses enumerated under
Appendix L.3; and
b.7) Explanation/justification on why payments for food and accommodation
of P311,610.00 and P333,900.00, respectively, were not supported by POs
and/or contract and paid out of the cash advance and not through checks
directly to the contractor/supplier.
c) City Sports Development Officer to require the Head of the Delegation, to
refund the amount of P9,000.00 representing payment for gas
consumption of the bus;
d) BAC Secretariat to submit the following documents of MME JJ Transport,
in compliance with Annex “H”, Appendix A of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA
No. 9184.
c.1 Mayors’s/Business Permit and DTI Registration or BIR Certificate of
Registration;
c.2 PhilGEPS Registration Number;
c.3 Income/Business Tax Return;
c.4 Omnibus Sworn Statements; and
c.5 Resolution recommending and justifying to the Head of Procuring
Entity (HOPE) the use of alternative mode of procurement and
approval by the HOPE of the BAC Resolution recommending award
of contract.
e) City Accountant to check thoroughly the completeness of supporting
documents before processing and approving the payment of
disbursements/claims.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
• Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit justification letter on why they
allowed and approved, during the evaluation of bids, the inclusion of
unnecessary work items in the detailed engineering design and estimates
submitted by the contractor; and
• Project Engineers to submit explanation on the differences of the installed
CCTV cameras as against the approved program of work and specifications;
and
• Assets Builders Corporation to refund the amount of P2,040,389.29 based
on COA-TSO Evaluation Report.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to:
a) review the procedures in the implementation of infrastructure projects by
administration and comply with the provision of Appendix 1 of RIRR of RA No.
9184 regarding rules and regulations on project implementation by
administration. The City may resort to Pakyaw Labor Contracting System or
procure the infrastructure projects through a straight contract that covered
labor and materials with the licensed contractor in accordance with RA 9184;
b) direct the project engineers to prepare and submit the Status of Work
Accomplished and As-Built plans These documents will be the basis for
the issuance of the Certificate of Completion and Acceptance by the Head
of the Agency;
c) submit contract entered into by and between the City Government and these
job orders;
d) provide explanations/justifications for the following: (a) cause of delay on the
payment of wages of the laborers in the implementation of the projects stated
above; (b) wide gap from receipt of materials to the implementation of the
project; (c) erasures in the all the DTRs and appointment of the laborers on the
project Improvement of Multi-purpose Building at Barangay Santor; and (d)
inclusion of the six laborers in the Program of Work as well as the propriety of
the 14 day’s work in the Installation of Lights and Timer at Barangay Trapiche
Gymnasium.

We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to ensure
the completeness and propriety of the required supporting documents for all
infrastructure projects implemented by administration before effecting payments.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Treasurer and the City Accountant to look into the root cause of the
delayed preparation and submission of the financial reports and disbursement
vouchers and take the necessary actions to ensure adequate measures that
will enable the City to meet such deadlines;
b. City Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed time frame for the
submission of monthly and quarterly reports as well as preparing the
corresponding transmittal letters for all receipts and disbursement records
with complete supporting documents being forwarded to the Office of the
Auditor; and
c. City Accountant to closely coordinate with the City Treasurer for the latter to
submit immediately the required reports within the prescribed period, and to
facilitate the recording of the transactions in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to submit and
furnish to the Audit Team not later than the 5th day of the ensuing month the
required CRAAF including the attached RAAFs as individual reports of all
accountable officers, pursuant to the guidelines provided in the MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume II.

We also recommended that the City Treasurer require the collectors and other
accountable officers to submit their individual Report of Accountability for
Accountable Forms at the end of the month as basis for the preparation and
submission of the CRAAF.

We recommended that City officials/employees adopt a system of filing,
safekeeping/preservation and disposal of government official records including
vouchers and accountability reports and accounting records in accordance with
the rules and regulations on Records Management and make use of the files for
their immediate reference/s. Likewise, observe the procedures stated in COA
Circular No. 2013-006 in the request for documents/records/reports/decisions and
other information in the possession and/or custody of COA.

We recommended that the Management maximize the utilization of the 70 percent
LDRRMF to enable the City to implement projects related to disaster prevention,
mitigation and preparedness within the scheduled timeframe to prevent possible
loss of life, damage to government and private property, and loss of livelihood.

We further recommended that the CDRRMC, with the help of the City Planning and
Development Office and City Engineering Office, resolve the identified issues or
problems encountered for the eventual implementation of the pending
programs/projects. By doing so, the City of Tanauan will be fully equipped and
can effectively respond during calamities.

Lastly, we recommended the reconciliation of the records being maintained for
the LDRRM Fund by the CDRRMO, Budget Office and Accounting Office to
ensure consistency and uniformity in data reporting.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the CDRRM Council:

• decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, and review and
update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to incorporate the PPAs to be
funded by the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years so
that the City becomes a disaster resilient community; and
• accomplish completely the LDRRMFIP by providing sufficient information in
the listed PPAs.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Development Council,
City Planning and Development Office, City Engineering Office and other
offices concerned in the implementation of projects under the 20 percent
Development Fund to strive in improving the planning, scheduling of work
activities and manpower in order to facilitate the timely completion of the
projects.

We also recommended that Management enhance the monitoring and
evaluation of project implementation to ensure optimum utilization of the 20
percent Development Fund and achieve the main purpose for which the fund
was intended.

We recommended that:

           a) The Local Chief Executive create an Inventory Committee  that
              will handle the annual  physical  count  of  inventories  and
              property plant and equipment, and preparation of  the  Report
              on the Physical Count of Inventories and Property, Plant  and
              Equipment;


           b) The Accounting Office coordinate with the Inventory Committee
              for the reconciliation of accounting records  on  inventories
              and PPE with the RPCI and RPCPPE;


           c) The Accounting Office to draw a journal entry  voucher  (JEV)
              to   set-up   an   allowance   for   depreciation    on   the
              municipality’s road networks and effect reclassification  and
              adjustments on  the  Flood  Control  System  and  Other  Land
              Improvements accounts to Road Networks account;  and


           d) The officers concerned strictly observe  the  accounting  and
              reporting   requirements   for   the   local   road   network
              construction and  maintenance  syste  as  prescribed  in  COA
              Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We recommended that the amount of Fidelity  bond  of  the  Accountable
      Officers be adjusted based on their total accountability  provided  in
      Annex C of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 and  that  cash  advances  of
      P445,450.00 granted for special purposes be liquidated immediately  as
      required in Section 89 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Municipality:

           1. Use the 70%  allocation  of  LDRRM  Fund  for  disaster  risk
              reduction  and  management  activities   only,   specifically
              related to the four thematic areas mandated in RA 10120; and

           2. Maintain a separate Registry for Quick Response Fund Item and
              Capital Outlay pursuant to 5.1.4 of COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended  that  management  ensure  implementation  and  optimal
      utilization of the 20% DF to help achieve the desirable socio-economic
      development and environmental outcomes of the LGU pursuant to DILG-DBM
      Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that to be  able  to  implement  waste  segregation  at
      source, the Municipality provide a separate collection  schedules  and
      or separate trucks or haulers for specific types of wastes,  otherwise
      the truck must have a suitable compartment for storing sorted waste in
      compliance with Section 22 and 24 of RA 9003 and its approved 10-year
      Solid Waste Management Plan.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Treasurer’s  Office  initiate  the
      quarterly release of the RPT shares  for  all  the  barangays  of  the
      Municipality pursuant to Section 271(d) of R.A. 7160.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System of the Municipality:

           a) Regularly monitor the implementation  of  the  GAD  Plan  and
              Budget  to  ensure  that  GAD  PPAs   are   effectively   and
              efficiently implemented to attain GAD objective; and

           b) Include only specific gender issues (with reference to women
              or girl children) / GAD mandates to be addressed and gender
              responsive programs, projects and activities in the GAD Plan
              and Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to draw a journal entry voucher to correct the understatement of the accounts, namely: Receipts from Market Operations; Permit Fees; Customers’ Deposit; and Other Receivables. Henceforth, strictly observe the proper use of the accounts in recording financial transactions in accordance with Section 111 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 for fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to comply with PPSAS 17 (IPSASs 17) by recognizing all completed projects as Property Plant and Equipment, regardless of fund source and billing received, by basing its cost from the Contract Agreement so that accurate PPE account will be reflected in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: Prepared adjusting entry thru: JEV No. 300-2019-01-018, 300-2019-05-134, and 300-2019-01-05

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the ensuing year, the Municipal Accountant observe the disclosure requirements on the investment, inventory, and financial liabilities accounts as provided for in paragraphs 75, and 127 of the PPSAS No. 1 (IPSAS 1) and paragraph 47 of PPSAS 12 (IPSAS 12), to achieve a fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: In the CY 2019 Notes to FS, Disclosure No. 7 for investment includes information about the composition on of the account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist maintain the required record book for proper monitoring and to facilitate inventory taking of agricultural implements received from DA-RFO8 and those procured by the municipality. We further recommend that the implementation of the program extended to the identified farmers’ association in Tanauan, Leyte be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement where conditions and responsibilities will be set out clearly.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Agriculturist is maintaining the required record books and programs extended to the farmers are covered with MOA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the disaster preparedness thematic area be given equal fund allocation with the other thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management. Likewise, consider the other equally important projects and activities under disaster preparedness such as, the conduct of simulation exercise to test plans and skills; development of education, information and communication campaign; etc., in the utilization of fund pursuant to Section 5.2 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 in order for the constituents to develop and strengthen their preparedness and capabilities in times of disaster.
observations.managementAction: Training such as first aid and basic life support training was conducted. With the meager funds for MDDRM, the MDRRMC have to allocate funds according to the priority needs of the municipality. Other activities on pre-disaster preparedness are included in their Disaster Preparedness Plan and are implemented through barangays and schools based campaign.
observations.reason: Audit Observation was issued for CY 2019 on the utilization on of MDRRM funds to expenses not related to the four thematic areas of the DRRM. Amounts could have been re-program to support disaster preparedness activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management prioritize and provide adequate funding for the construction of the Sanitary Landfill pursuant to Section 37 of Republic Act No. 9003 to hasten the closure of the open dumpsite and to ensure the protection of public health.
observations.managementAction: The LGU through its Solid Waste Management Board formulated a work plan for waste segregation from source up to dumpsite closure. However, implementation of the work plan will be on CY 2020.
observations.reason: Per ocular inspection, only the segregation at source in the pilot areas and the construction of the Materials Recovery Facility at Brgy. Lapay was implemented. An AOM for CY 2019 was issued for unsegregated collection of wastes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that lease agreement between the Municipality and market vendors be supported with duly signed and notarized Lease Agreement Contract pursuant to Section 31 of the LGU’s Tax Ordinance No. 2017-09. We also recommended for the strict adherence to policies and guidelines set forth in their ordinance particularly on securing business permit before occupancy, compliance to advance payment and deposit, prompt payment of monthly rentals, sealing of weighing scales and keeping of fire extinguisher in the rented premises. Otherwise, enforce the sanctions, surcharges and penalties provided in Section 24 and 53 of the Ordinance.
observations.managementAction: Market vendors are covered with Lease Agreement contracts and Management see to it that existing policies and guidelines set forth in their ordinance are strictly followed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System meet regularly in order to evaluate GAD Fund utilization, monitor its implementation and provide assistance to achieve a hundred percent implementation of all PPAs identified in the GAD Plan and Budget in compliance to Section 36 of R.A. No. 9710 and Joint Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA. We also recommend the strict compliance on the submission of GAD Accomplishments within the required deadline set in COA Circular 2014-01 using the format prescribed in Annex E of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Copy of revised format has not yet been submitted to COA. However, GAD fund utilization for CY 2019 though not fully utilized showed improvement from 33.62% to 64% implementation in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accounting and treasurer’s division be required to strictly remit to BIR the full amount withheld every due date.
observations.managementAction: Taxes withheld are remitted timely on a regular basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer reconcile her cashbook balances every end of the month or as the need arises with the subsidiary accounting records as required in Section 63 (F) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.
observations.managementAction: Updating of cashbook and accounting records.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of cashbook and accounting records on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management file the request for authority to write-off the dormant account totaling P405,706.01 to the COA Audit Team pursuant to Section 8 of COA Circular No. 2016-005, dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Ongoing process on gathering the documentary requirement such as death certificate of the deceased accountable officers for attachment to request for write-off of CA totaling P129,846.01. Only the request for authority to write-off inter-agency receivable accounts have been filed to COA Audit Team. Documentary requirements for write-off of CA totaling P129,846.01 had not yet been submitted to COA Audit Team.
observations.reason: Only the request for authority to write-off inter-agency receivable accounts have been filed to COA Audit Team. Documentary requirements for write-off of CA totaling P129,846.01 had not yet been submitted to COA Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipality maximize utilization of appropriated funds in implementing the various programs for risk reduction and management in order to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities. Likewise, require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to transfer to the Special Trust Fund the unexpended balances of LDRRMF for CY 2016 and CY 2017 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: Utilization of funds for CY 2019 registered at 52%, and this audit observation has been reiterated in CY 2019.
observations.reason: Utilization of funds for CY 2019 registered at 52%, and this audit observation has been reiterated in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management appropriate in the LGU’s Annual Budget in the ensuing year, 1% of its IRA for the strengthening and implementation of the programs, projects and activities of their Local Council for Protection of Children as required by RA 9344 and reiterated in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.managementAction: More than 1% has been appropriated in the ensuing year, 1% of its IRA for protection of children thru its GAD Plan and Budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management fast track the enforcement of the law on waste segregation at source and other programs/activities and strategies incorporated in their 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan in compliance with Republic Act 9003, dated January 26, 2001.
observations.managementAction: Started the implementation of its PPAs embodied in their 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan such as: Segregation at source with the pilot Town Housing, Public Market, Municipal Hall and Public Plaza as the pilot sites. Started the construction of the Materials Recovery Facility at Brgy. Lapay. Procured a bio-reactor. Formulated a work plan for waste segregation program of the Municipality.
observations.reason: Per ocular inspection, only the segregation at source in the pilot areas and the construction of the Materials Recovery Facility at Brgy. Lapay was implemented. An AOM for CY 2019 was issued for unsegregated collection of wastes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management assign a committee to conduct and submit the physical inventory of its property, plant and equipment.
observations.managementAction: Inventory was submitted on PPE by department but does not reconcile with the accounting record. Inventory Committee has yet to be organized.
observations.reason: Report on the Physical count of PPE did not include the land, land improvements, infrastructure, building and other structures totaling P155M per accounting record. Likewise, the submitted report was presented per office not consolidated and not per PPE line item.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant keep complete subsidiary ledgers and make proper disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements in strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Accountant maintains subsidiary ledgers for Trust Liabilities – DRRM Fund and disclosures related thereto are included in the Notes to FS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the Inventories Held for Distribution and for Consumption according to the correct quantity on hand and its valuation as of year-end supported with inventory reports and supplies ledger cards.
observations.managementAction: Inventory report was submitted by the Supply Officer however, it does not reconcile with the recorded balance with the Accounting Office. Included as audit observation for CY 2019.
observations.reason: Inventory report was submitted by the Supply Officer however, it does not reconcile with the recorded balance with the Accounting Office. Included as audit observation for CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management immediately cause the return the unutilized financial assistance to DOST amounting to P2,908,134.00 in accordance with the provisions of the MOA, considering the lapse of four (4) years from the time the financial assistance was received by the LGU.
observations.managementAction: The Water Filter Production Equipment was acquired and paid. P2,023,800.00. The Water Filter Production has a remaining balance of P259,548.36 and still with LGU – Tanauan. Unutilized funds were returned to DOST on 11/6/18 per OR No. 7653079.
observations.reason: The Water Filter Production Equipment was acquired and paid. Remaining balance of P259,548.36 still with LGU – Tanauan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the above listed employees to immediately return the funds of the LGU in the total amount of P6,141.34.
observations.reason: All of the concerned receivables were collected except for the account of Asuncion Mosca who is on indefinite leave due to illness.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the concerned employees to refund the total amount received for the Emergency Cash Assistance, Calamity Rehabilitation Assistance and PEI for CY 2014 in the total amount of P4,730,531.62.
observations.managementAction: Only Officials and employees separated from the LGU were required to refund the PEI. The PEI is now a subject of a Notice of Disallowance which has been appealed by the Tanauan Association of Municipal Employees, hence refund is pending for those in active service.
observations.reason: Concerned PEI is now a subject of a Notice of Disallowance which has been appealed by the Tanauan Association of Municipal Employees, hence refund is pending for those in active service.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the disbursement vouchers, payrolls, official receipts and financial reports pursuant to Section 6.5 of COA Cir. No. 95-006 and Section 100 of PD 1445.
observations.managementAction: All the transactions documents were submitted to COA. Although delayed due to the devastation wrought by ST Yolanda, the Office of the Municipal Accountant were able to submit all the required reports and transaction documents. In addition, management re-submitted copy of the Damage Report to justify why there are missing proofs of submission for certain months.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Mayor should require the officials concerned to strictly observe Section 20 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs, Vol. I. He should direct the Municipal treasurer to prioritize the updating of the RPTARs and come up with the certified list of taxpayers showing the realty taxes due so that the Municipal Accountant shall have a correct basis in the setting up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year.
observations.managementAction: Updating of RPTAR is still on the process. Treasurer’s Office is still on the process of reconstructing its records. Award of the contract for the computerization has yet to be made by the BAC.
observations.reason: Treasurer’s Office is still on the process of reconstructing its records.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the City Mayor require the:
· creation of an Inventory Committee who will conduct a complete and accurate physical
count of all properties found at station and being used by the City in its operations
including those properties acquired before CY 2008;
· Inventory Committee to prepare the reports using the prescribed forms, the
RPCPPE for the Report on the physical count of PPE by type as of given date and Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for unserviceable properties, copy
furnished the COA Auditor;
· City Accountant and the City General Services Officer (GSO) to exert more efforts
to reconcile their records on PPE and identify/investigate causes of the noted
discrepancies in the balances of accounts to bring the two records in agreement; and
effect the necessary adjustments in both records, if warranted; and
· City GSO to take immediate action to prepare and submit to the
Disposal/Appraisal Committee a duly accomplished IIRUP and the Waste Material
Report together with the other documentary requirements to facilitate the
determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the unserviceable
property as required under Section 79 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 to
prevent exposure to different elements and further deterioration of its values.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) City General Services Officer (GSO) to prepare and maintain Road Networks
Property Cards;
b) City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the City GSO with the complete
description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; and
c) City Accountant to prepare and maintain Property Ledger Cards for Local Road
Networks and disclose the information for the said property in the Notes to
Financial Statements in accordance with the prescribed format as provided under
COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We recommended that the City Mayor make representations with the representative of
the Catholic Bishop of Lipa for possible donation/purchase of the subject land where
school buildings lies, and to require the:
a) City Budget Officer to request from Sangguniang Panglungsod to appropriate
funds for the titling of land owned by the City;
b) City Assessor and the City Treasurer to facilitate the transfer/registration of all
real estate owned by the City under its name to protect the interest of the
government pursuant to Section 47(c) of the Rules and Regulations Implementing
the Local Government Code of 1991 and Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386;
c) City GSO to conduct and prepare a complete, accurate and updated Inventory of
Land owned by the City and to furnish the Accounting Office the list/records of
the 49 parcels of land as well as copies of all the supporting documents such as
tax declaration and deed of sale, for the latter’s basis of recording in its books of accounts; and
d) City General Services Office, the City Assessor and the City Accountant to
reconcile their records/schedules to come up with a reliable and accurate
inventory balance of Land.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the TCC Administrator to:
· direct the Accountable Officer of TCC to transfer/remit the balance of the
grants/fund transfer, scheduled to be utilized in Academic Year (AY) 2019, to the
City Treasurer for deposit, and the City Accountant to maintain under a separate
subsidiary ledger in the General Fund of the City. Henceforth, remit all
collections/fund transfers to the City Treasurer for deposit to the authorized
depository bank. Likewise, all claims for payments, disbursements, cash
advances and procurement activities should be forwarded to the City, for the
usual processing using the prescribed accounting forms and for
certification/approval by the authorized officials of the City;
· instruct the Acting Accountant of TCC to furnish the City Accountant with the
2018 financial reports for recording of the forwarding balances in financial
statements of the City; and
· refrain from drawing cash advance for purposes of paying other
miscellaneous expenses/procurement of goods.
We further recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant, the School
Administrator and all other concerned personnel to follow the process flow of
collections and disbursement of funds in accordance with the MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to ensure that
cash advances be granted only for specific purpose and for expenses which are
difficult, impractical or impossible to be paid by check aside from ensuring that the
government treasury received the mandatory collections from business enterprises.
We recommended that the Management strictly comply with the provisions provided
in RA No. 9184 and its IRR, and COA Circular No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the Management submit written justification of the
necessity of paying the transactions in cash through a cash advance instead through
the issuance of check.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer and City Accountant
with the help of CSWD Office and City Cooperative Office to:
· intensify collections of Loans Receivable, designate key personnel who will
monitor the loans granted and enforce payment thereof. Otherwise, resort to
appropriate legal remedies;
· establish alternative payment schemes and plans to enforce efficient collection
from defaulting borrowers; and
· set up clear-cut policies and guidelines on the loans granted out of Cooperative
Support Fund, Capital Assistance for Cooperative and SMEs, Self-Employment
Assistance (SEA) and Microfinance Fund Programs. The beneficiaries, amounts
and documentary requirements and the processing office/s should be well defined
to ensure that only qualified borrowers/cooperatives are assisted and that
assistance is given in line with the purpose and objectives of the aforesaid
programs.

We also recommended that request for write-off of receivables be filed with the
Commission on Audit in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016 - 005 dated
December 19, 2016 should collection be not feasible despite diligent efforts and after
exhausting all available remedies. Submission of all proofs that efforts have been
initiated to collect the same should be undertaken.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
· City Accountant and Treasurer to submit immediately the issued accountable
forms/official receipts to the Audit Team to facilitate the review and verification of
the same, otherwise the transactions will be suspended in audit. Henceforth,
ensure that said collection reports are submitted within the prescribed period; and
· City Treasurer to instruct the Cashier/Liquidating Officer to accomplish/fill-up
Section C of the Report of Collections and Deposits pertaining to the reports on the
accountability for accountable forms which shall include the name of forms and
number, beginning balance, received, issued and ending balance.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant in coordination with
City HRMD Office to:
· regularly reconcile the balances in accounting records with the records of the
GSIS in order to ascertain the reliability of the amount Due to GSIS in the financial
statements; and
· effect timely and accurate remittance of GSIS premium contributions and loan
payments.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and all Accountable
Officers to:
§ stop immediately the wrongful and irregular practice of delayed and partial
deposits of collections;
§ deposit all collections intact daily or not later than the next banking day to avoid
the risk of loss of government money. All cash in the custody should be deposited
immediately with the Authorized Government Depository Bank; and
§ enforce strictly Section 307 of RA No. 7160, Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and Section
15 MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, to avoid accumulation of funds in the possession
of the Liquidating Officer and avert risk of loss or misuse of government funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to require the
Liquidating Officer to:
· sign in the DSCAF whenever the collections together with the duplicate and
triplicate copies of used official receipts were turned over to him by the
collectors;
· prepare and sign the RCD; and
· remit intact cash collections to the Treasurer/Cashier for deposit to the authorized
depository bank.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer/Cashier to
deposit all collections intact daily or on the next banking day to avert possible loss or
misuse of the funds, and henceforth require them to perform the procedures for the
receipt, reporting and verification of collections and accountable forms in accordance
with Sections 30, 31 and 32, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor strictly comply with the provisions of Joint
Circular No. 2015-01 of the COA, DBM, DILG, Governance Commission of the GOCC,
and DND on the use of Confidential Funds.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant, and other
local officials concerned to submit to the Audit Team, for review and verification, the
disbursement voucher for the grant of cash advance for confidential expense,
together with all the required supporting documents, and the corresponding Journal
Entry Voucher (JEV) upon its liquidation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer
with the help of the City Cooperative Office to:
· issue demand letters to TPMMPC member – borrowers thru its officers for the
collection of their outstanding balance and remit immediately the collections to
the Source Agency; and
· follow-up the status of the City’s request to the Office of the President that
repayment of the outstanding balance of P366,666.67 be waived.

We recommended that the City Social Welfare and Development Officer (CSWDO), the
City Accountant and Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) strictly comply with the
aforementioned laws, rules and regulations, specifically:
· Follow strictly the Agreement of the Supplemental Feeding Program and include
in the project proposal the specific dates of feeding to be conducted and the
names of the target children/beneficiaries for every Day Care Center in the
locality. Further, secure daily attendance list with photographs duly certified by
the Day Care Worker In-charge in the conduct of supplemental feeding and attach
the same to the Liquidation Report to establish propriety of the claims;
· Require the immediate submission of the lacking documents as discussed in the
said audit observation;
· Cause the opening of a bank account solely for the transfer of funds for the DWSD
Supplementary Feeding Program; and
· Fast track the implementation of the program so as not to require the beneficiaries
for the School Years 2018-2019 supplemental feeding program from attending
school even on their summer vacation, and to be able to implement on time the
new Supplemental Feeding Program on the start of School Years 2019-2010 for
the new beneficiaries.

We further recommended that the CSWDO prepare its procurement plan for the SFP
according to the prescribed menu by the Nutritionist or the DSWD to serve as basis
for the food purchases.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CSWD Officer, City Accountant and
the City Treasurer to:
· complete the required information in the submitted payrolls by submitting copies
of OSCA IDs, with signature or fingerprint of the beneficiaries, in order to validate
the authenticity and accuracy of paid payrolls amounting to P9,420,000.00;
· ensure that liquidation reports for payment of Social Pension are supported with
properly accomplished payrolls duly signed by the recipients and all authorized
signatories; and
· see to it that only the actual amount disbursed for the quarter is reported in the
liquidation report and deposit the unclaimed stipend of first and third quarter
payments amounting to P75,000.00 to the Trust Account for DSWD Social
Pension.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City HRM Officer, City Accountant,
City Budget Officer, City Planning and Development Coordinator and TCGEU
President to submit the following:
· Certification and/or documents that will show that said savings were generated
out of improvement/streamlining of systems and procedures and cost-cutting
measures;
· Report/Summary/Schedule showing percentage (%) of accomplishments against
targeted programs/activities/projects as prescribed in Item 4.1.2 of DBM Budget
Circular No. 2018-5 dated November 14, 2018 (Accomplishment Report on targeted
PPAs approved in the Annual Budget for CY 2018); and
· The internal guidelines to be followed in the grant of the CNA Incentive, such as: i)
the specific criteria for determining who are entitled, and ii) the distribution of the
amount available and the rate of the CNA Incentive in accordance with item 5.1.2
of the same DBM Circular.

We also recommended that the City Administrator, City Accountant and TCGEU
President identify in the CNA the cost-cutting measures and system improvement
that the management and the union have to undertake in order to meet and deliver the
planned targets, programs or services at a lesser cost.

We further recommended that the complete supporting documents as required are
already attached to the payroll before payment of CNA Incentive pursuant to Budget
Circular No. 2018-5 to avoid audit suspensions and disallowances.

We recommended that the Tanauan City PRAISE Committee submit immediately the
following documents in order to validate whether or not the City officials and
employees have met all the requirements warranting the grant of the mentioned
awards and incentives:
A. Organizational Performance Incentive (OPI):
· Evaluation reports or any documentation recognizing suggestions, inventions,
superior accomplishments and other personal efforts which resulted in monetary
savings making the City officials and employees eligible for PRAISE award; and
· Report detailing the savings generated during CY 2018 which prompted the grant
of OPI and other relevant supporting documents.
B. Loyalty Incentives:
Loyalty Incentives of P410,000.00
· Certification from HRO that the claimant has not incurred more than 50 days
authorized vacation leave without pay within the 10-year period or aggregate of
more than 25 days authorized vacation leave without pay within the 5-year period,
as the case may be, as required under CSC Memorandum Circular No. 06, s. 2002.
Additional Loyalty Incentives of P364,000.00
· Specific authority/legal basis for the grant of the additional loyalty incentives to its
officials and employees.
C. Gawad Paglilingkod Award
· Employees’ individual appraisal or evaluation reports or any proof that the
grantees have satisfactory ratings for the last two rating periods preceding the
retirement.
D. Local Scholarship Program (LSP)
· Copy of disbursement voucher and its supporting documents under check no.
679488 dated December 04, 2018 amounting to P502,500.00 for the partial
payment to LPU Laguna for the MPA Program;
· Copy of guidelines/criteria for the selection/screening of beneficiaries for the local
scholarship program; and
· Specific authority/legal basis for the grant of scholarships in the pursuit of higher
education as an employee incentive under PRAISE.

We recommended that the Management stop the grant of additional loyalty incentives
more than the limit imposed in CSC MC No. 06, s. 2002.

We also recommended that Management comply with the existing rules and
regulations of governing bodies like COA, DBM, and CSC and others in the
utilization of government resources.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
· Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), City Planning and Development Officer
(CPDO), City Engineer, City Budget Officer and City Accountant to ensure the
conduct of pre-procurement conference before the Procuring Entity start with the
procurement process;
· BAC and BAC Technical Working Group (TWG) to:
- provide an explanation for the inconsistencies in the different phases of the
procurement process;
- ensure at all times that winning bidders post performance securities within the
prescribed period to make certain that the interests of the government is
adequately covered in the event it is established that a winning bidder
fails to perform any of its obligations under the contract;
- require the winning bidders to submit performance securities with a validity
period up to final acceptance or after the defects liability period, and if
there are contract time extensions, require the contractor to submit the
corresponding extension of the security’s validity period; and
- be more cautious in evaluating the legal, technical and financial documents
submitted by bidders during bid evaluation and post-qualification.
· City Accountant to ensure the posting of warranty securities in the payment of
infrastructure projects by the contractors and demand for liquidated damages
from erring contractors for delayed completion of work.
· City Engineer to:
- direct the respective contractors of the eighteen (18) infrastructure projects
with unsubmitted As-Built Plan (see Annex Q.2) to immediately prepare and
submit the same to the Office of the City Auditor; and
- perform sufficient planning and detailed engineering activities and ensure
that issues such as site problems, concerns by local/national government
units and other matters that could delay project implementation are
resolved prior to procurement of infrastructure contracts.
· City Treasurer to inform Keascia Construction and Supply to issue Official
Receipts for the following projects:
a) Improvement of Road at Barangay Poblacion I;
b) Construction/Improvement of Drainage at Barangay Pagaspas;
c) Supply & delivery of labor, materials & equipment for Barangay
Pagaspas; and
d) Construction of Drainage System at Barangay Janopol Occidental

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC)
and Technical Working Group (TWG) to:
· scrutinize strictly the sufficiency of the bidder’s manpower and equipment on all
the projects to be awarded and not on an individual project only in cases where
the bidder was awarded several projects which have similar or overlapping
implementation period. This is to oblige the winning contractor to supplement its
manpower and/or equipment to be utilized in the projects awarded to him or to
disqualify him from obtaining several awards of contracts in conformity with the
provisions of Sections 23.6, 34.1 and 34.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
· strictly evaluate during bid evaluation and post qualification the List of All
Ongoing Government and Private Contracts and whether or not the submitted Net
Financial Contracting Capacity (NFCC) of the bidders who were awarded several
projects accurately reflect its financial capacity to carry out the work sought to be
undertaken; and
· monitor contractors with ongoing projects with other government agencies and
coordinate with those involved in implementing infrastructure projects, to
determine undisclosed projects in the Statement of Ongoing and Completed
Projects to avoid overlapping implementation and delay in project
accomplishment, and/or substandard quality of work.

We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Engineer to strictly and
closely monitor all projects simultaneously implemented by the contractors and
prepare an individual status report per project to be submitted to the City Mayor for
monitoring purposes and in order to check, verify and evaluate the quality of work
and efficiency of the said contractors.

We recommended that the City School Board improve SEF utilization performance by
maximizing the use of available funds to implement various Program Projects and
Activities (PPAs) allowed to be charged against the SEF under the existing laws, rules and
regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
· BAC Secretariat to ensure the posting of warranty security in accordance with
Section 62.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184;
· BAC to submit a written justification why the submitted Tax Clearance of
Gawanato Construction was not validated during Bid evaluation and Post-
Qualification process, which resulted in the award of contract to ineligible
bidder. Enforce strictly the sanctions against the erring contractor for
submitting spurious TCC, and BAC and BAC TWG for not conducting bid
evaluation and post-qualification in accordance with Section 34, Rule X of the
2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
· City Engineer to direct Arsvico Construction and Development Corporation to
prepare and submit the As-Built Plan for the Construction of two storey six
classroom school building at Natatas Elementary School, Barangay Natatas,
Tanauan City;
· City Treasurer to inform Keascia Construction and Supply to issue Official
Receipts for the following projects: a) Construction of mini-gymnasium with
stage at Santol E/S, Barangay Santol; and b) Full payment of Construction of
two storey six classroom school building at Tapia Elementary School,
Barangay VI; and
· BAC and BAC TWG to be more cautious in evaluating the legal, technical and
financial documents submitted by bidders during preliminary evaluation, bid
evaluation and post-qualification.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to:
· require personnel/employees of Treasurers Office to conduct a thorough/rigid
inspection/proper scrutiny of the AFs upon receipt thereof, under the supervision
of the AO;
· instruct the checker-employees who will conduct inspection to affix their
initials/signatures in the booklet of AF in order to pinpoint whose
responsibility/accountability, in case of discrepancy;
· review the procedures followed in the notification/circularization of the loss of
accountable forms in accordance with COA Circular No. 84-233 dated August 21,
1984; and
· return the unissued sets of defective AFs/ORs to the NPO, for replacement and
henceforth ensure that a sound system of internal control, in the receipt and
issuance of AFs, is in place.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to:
§ compel the Accountable Officer to prepare a correct and reliable Treasurer’s
Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (TRAAF) and to submit
140
the same together with the RAAF of Collectors, to the Office of the Audit Team
Leader not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month;
§ review the TRAAF prepared by the Supply Accountable Officer;
§ submit to the Office of the Audit Team Leader the duplicate copies of the 4,562
accountable forms/Official Receipts issued together with the Report of Collections
and Deposits;
§ explain in writing why the 4,562 pcs. accountable forms reported as issued in the
Treasurer’s Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms were not
included/reported in the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD);
§ oblige the SAO for accountable forms to properly accomplished her
cashbook/logbook of accountable forms and require the collectors to sign in the
logbook and affix the date whenever AFs were issued to them; and
§ submit the TRAAF for the period August to December 2018 and the Report of all
Cancelled Accountable Forms.

We further recommended that henceforth, Management shall ensure that all reports
for accountable forms are correct, complete, adequately supported by documents
and timely submitted to the Office of the Audit Team Leader, to serve as reference for
audit and for monitoring purposes.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
· set up special accounts for Tanauan City Colleges (TCC), Slaughter House and
Market Operations, 20 per cent Development Fund and Long Term Loans with
LBP; and
· maintain separate and complete subsidiary ledgers and prepare and submit year-
end trial balances and financial statements for the special accounts to afford the
City adequate information on the results of their operations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to countersign
all the checks drawn by the City Treasurer pursuant to Section 345 of RA No. 7160
and Section 43 of COA Circular No. 92-382.

1.12. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to draw
a journal entry voucher to correct the understatement of the accounts,
namely: Receipts from Market Operations; Permit Fees; Customers’
Deposit; and Other Receivables. Henceforth, strictly observe the proper use
of the accounts in recording financial transactions in accordance with Section
30
111 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 for
fair presentation in the financial statements.

2.10 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to
comply with PPSAS 17 (IPSASs 17) by recognizing all completed projects as
Property Plant and Equipment, regardless of fund source and billing
received, by basing its cost from the Contract Agreement so that accurate
PPE account will be reflected in the Statement of Financial Position.

3.9 We recommended that in the ensuing year, the Municipal Accountant
observe the disclosure requirements on the investment, inventory, and
financial liabilities accounts as provided for in paragraphs 75, and 127 of the
PPSAS No. 1 (IPSAS 1) and paragraph 47 of PPSAS 12 (IPSAS 12), to achieve
a fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

4.9 We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist maintain the
required record book for proper monitoring and to facilitate inventory
taking of agricultural implements received from DA-RFO8 and those
procured by the municipality. We further recommend that the
implementation of the program extended to the identified farmers’
association in Tanauan, Leyte be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement
where conditions and responsibilities will be set out clearly.

5.10 We recommended that the disaster preparedness thematic area be
given equal fund allocation with the other thematic areas of disaster risk
reduction and management. Likewise, consider the other equally important
projects and activities under disaster preparedness such as, the conduct of
simulation exercise to test plans and skills; development of education,
information and communication campaign; etc., in the utilization of fund
pursuant to Section 5.2of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-1 in order for the constituents to develop and strengthen
their preparedness and capabilities in times of disaster.

6.7 We recommended that management prioritize and provide adequate
funding for the construction of the Sanitary Landfill pursuant to Section 37
of Republic Act No. 9003 to hasten the closure of the open dumpsite and to
ensure the protection of public health.

7.9 We recommended that lease agreement between the Municipality and
market vendors be supported with duly signed and notarized Lease
Agreement Contract pursuant to Section 31 of the LGU’s Tax Ordinance No.
2017-09. We also recommend for the strict adherence to policies and
guidelines set forth in said Tax Ordinance particularly on securing business
permit before occupancy, compliance to advance payment and deposit,
prompt payment of monthly rentals, sealing of weighing scales and keeping
42
of fire extinguisher in the rented premises. Otherwise, enforce the sanctions,
surcharges and penalties provided in Section 24 and 53 of the Ordinance.

8.14 We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System meet regularly in
order to evaluate GAD Fund utilization, monitor its implementation and
provide assistance to achieve a hundred percent implementation of all PPAs
identified in the GAD Plan and Budget in compliance to Section 36 of R.A.
No. 9710 and Joint Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and
NEDA. We also recommend the strict compliance on the submission of GAD
Accomplishments within the required deadline set in COA Circular 2014-01
using the format prescribed in Annex E of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA
JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016.

9.10 We recommended that the accounting and treasurer’s division be
required to strictly remit to BIR the full amount withheld every due date.

21. We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the disbursement vouchers, payrolls, official receipts and financial reports pursuant to Section 6.5 of COA Cir. No. 95-006 and Section 100 of PD 1445.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer reconcile her cashbook
balances every end of the month or as the need arises with the subsidiary accounting
records as required in Section 63 (F) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommend that an Appraisal Committee be created to appraise the
donated equipment so that these properties could be recorded in the books and
provide adequate disclosure in the Notes to Combined Financial Statements as
required in PPSAS 1 and 17. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant and the
Property Officer to prepare the PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards,
respectively.

We recommend that management file the request for authority to write-off
the dormant account totaling P405,706.01 to the COA Audit Team pursuant to
Section 8 of COA Circular No. 2016-005, dated December 19, 2016.

We recommend that the municipality maximize utilization of appropriated
funds in implementing the various programs for risk reduction and management in
order to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities. Likewise, require
the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to transfer to the Special Trust
Fund the unexpended balances of LDRRMF for CY 2016 and CY 2017 pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommend that management appropriate in the LGU’s Annual Budget in
the ensuing year, 1% of its IRA for the strengthening and implementation of
programs, projects and activities of their Local Council for Protection of Children
as required by RA 9344 and reiterated in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-
120 dated July 4, 2012.

We recommend that the Local School Board be directed to re-evaluate its
School Board Budget and for the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with
Section 272 of RA 7160 and DepED, DBM, DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017
particularly on the utilization of SEF to avoid inappropriate charges to the fund.
Likewise, require the Local School Board, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal
Budget Officer to use the prescribe forms for SEF Budget Preparation and SEF
Budget Accountability.

We recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly comply with the
required documents for the alternative method of procurement as provided for in
Annex H of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 and
Appendix, thereof.

We recommend that management fast track the enforcement of the law on
waste segregation at source and other programs/activities and strategies
incorporated in their Solid Waste Management Plan in compliance with Republic
Act 9003, dated January 26, 2001.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

¢ the City Accountant to ensure that all small value assets with useful life of
more than one year but costing less than £10,000.00 are properly
classified and recorded accordingly and to prepare journal entries and
effect the necessary adjustments on the PPE, Accumulated Depreciation
and Government Equity accounts for a fair presentation of the accounts
in the financial statements;

* the General Services Officer (GSO) to issue the corresponding Inventory
Custodian Slip (ICS) to all the end-users of the small tangible items and
to cancel the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE)
previously issued;

¢ the GSO and the Accounting Office to conduct detailed review of the
documents maintained to determine the correct classification of each PPE
and the consequent classification and adjustments to ensure the accuracy
of both records;

the GSO to take immediate action to facilitate the appraisal and disposal
of the abovementioned unserviceable properties as required by
Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 to prevent exposure to different
elements and further deterioration of its value;

o the City Accountant to draw journal entry on the sale of disposed
property;

o creation of an Inventory Committee who will conduct an accurate
physical count of all properties found at station and being used by the
City in its operations including those properties acquired before CcYy
2008; and

o the Committee to reconcile the result of the physical count of all PPE with
the property and accounting records and submit to the Auditor not later
than January 31, of each year.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Accountant to
provide Subsidiary Ledger (SL) complete with details/information for each on-
going project to facilitate monitoring of payment of individual project. Further,
effect the necessary adjustment in the book of accounts to present accurate
account balance.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the
corresponding Heads of the Offices of Treasury, General Services, City Health
and City Veterinary to recoucile the differences noted in order to present
accurate balance of the said accounts at any given period. Further, after
reconciliation, require the City Accountant and the different Accountable
Officers/Department Heads concerned to prepare and record the adjustment in
the Supplies Ledger Card/Stock Card accordingly, when necessary.

Likewise, require them to properly maintain complete and updated inventory
ledger cards and inventory stock cards per class/category of property as a means
of control. Reconcile the said records regularly to protect the integrity of
information being reported in the financial statements of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor oblige the General Service Officer
(GSO) to secure and immediately submit the necessary documentary
requirements as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 to legitimize
ownership of the land purchased and to initiate proper titling of the same to
avoid exposure to third party claim and protect the interest of the City
Government of Tanauan. Further, the City Accountant to refrain from
processing claim with incomplete documentation.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned Contractor to
undertake the necessary repair of the visible defects/cracks and completion of
unfinished works noted during the ocular inspection to prevent further damage
pursuant to Section 62.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184, Further, require the
concerned City Project Engineer, who issued the Certificate of Inspection to be
100% completed, and in accordance with the approved plans, program of work
and specification, those identified projects with unfinished works to submit
written explanation/justification to include comment on whether or not the
above lapses constitute a violation of RA No. 9184 and its RIRR.

We recommended that the City Treasurer procure the accountable forms
directly from recognized government printers such as Bangko Sentral ng
Pilipinas, National Printing Office, and Apo Production Unit, Inc. as strictly
mandated by the GPPB and DILG in order to protect the interest of the
government. Further, when necessary, seek the assistance of the Presidential
Communications Operations Office in determining whether no RGP is capable
of undertaking the printing service before engaging the services of private
printer.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator und the
Officer-In-Charge (OIC) of the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
(CDRRMO) and other department heads to take full cognizance of their principal
responsibility and accountability relative to the prudent use of scarce financial
resources of the government in conducting similar activities as prescribed of
DILG Circular No. 2011-59. Further, direct the OIC-CDRRM and the
concerned participants who did not attend the training to refund the equivalent
amount of rooms and meals paid by the government in their behalf.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:

a, stop the practice of recording/charging regular purchases of supplies and
materials outright to the expense accounts and follow the Perpetual
Inventory Method of accounting inventories;

b. ensure that issuances thereof shall be recorded monthly based on the Report
of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) prepared by the General Services
Office (GSO);

<. maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs) for each inventory stock; and

d. reconcile the SLCs with the inventory balance of that of the GSO to
ensure correctness of account balances at any given period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point Person to:

a. properly monitor and report regularly the status of implementation of the
activities stated in the Gender and Development Plan in order to address
gender issues;

b. ensure that the program and projects included in the GAD Plan are gender
responsive, processes and procedures are properly followed, and other
factors that would affect the implementation are considered to avoid
problems when it comes to implementation; and

c. utilize the fund solely for GAD related activities or to specific programs that
are gender-responsive and are aligned to the GAD mandate.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to strictly
enforce the submission of required accounting and financial reports within the
prescribed period pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of the RRSA prescribed by COA
Circular No. 2009-006 and Section 122 of PD No. 1445.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned officers to
look into the root cause of the delay in the preparation and submission of the
financial reports and effect appropriate action/s when necessary, to make
possible the early submission of the same.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC Secretariat to:

1. prepare the Consolidated Annual Procurement Plan for the procurement of
goods and infrastructure projects to conform with the provisions of RA No.
9184; and

2. resort to alternative mode of procurements invariably applying the
requirements prescribed in the same RIRR only when there are evidences to
support the conclusion that public bidding cannot be ultimately pursued.

January 30, 2013.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
require the Bids and Awards Committee, BAC Secretariat and BAC Technical Working Group to submit all the lacking documents noted to support the propriety of the procurement made by the City Government; and
direct the City Health Officer to ensure that all drugs and medicines to be procured are included in the PNDF, otherwise, a written request with justification should be submitted to the Head of the National Policy Staff for approval of the purchase to avoid disallowance in audit; instruct the BAC Secretariat to post all the scheduled biddings in conspicuous places in the City, in addition to postings in the City website and in PhilGEPS, to inform the prospective Suppliers/Contractors of the scheduled bidding, and attach a certification in the voucher that posting was made in conspicuous places; and direct the BAC to verify and validate all documents submitted by the Suppliers/Contractors before declaring a winning bidder.
observations.managementAction:
The City Health Office follows the PNDF, however, medicines that are included in COA findings were requested by different offices who requested them. With that incidence, they promised that they will retrieve the documents. On the other hand, the pharmacist accepted her inaccuracy.

The City has followed-up procurement posting and BAC verification procedures.
observations.reason: No Management comment yet

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor establish the Internal Audit Unit/Division and ensure its staffing and functionality.
observations.managementAction: The City already created the Internal Audit Division under the Office of the City Mayor, but there is no applicant yet for the created position.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Management require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the deposits of the LGU to the correct account Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Time Deposits (1-02-01-010).

observations.reason: According to the Municipal Accountant, the conversion of deposits from NGAS to PPSAS were merely rolled over on a monthly basis. Per advise by the speaker during the orientation, these kind of deposits should only be treated as savings deposits.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Management require the Municipal Accountant to record the purchases of supplies using the perpetual inventory system.
observations.managementAction: AAR CYs 2014 2009 Municipal Finding Nos. 5 & 3
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant continues to employ direct expense method in view of the fact that supplies are directly delivered to the end-users and are not being held for stock at the Supply Office due to lack of storage space and adequate personnel.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Management refund the total amount of P4,730,531.62 for the Emergency Cash Assistance, Calamity Rehabilitation Assistance and PEI for CY 2014 in the total amount of P4,730,531.62.
observations.managementAction: Not Issued ND No. 2016-002(14) dated April 23, 2016.

observations.reason: Management promised that they will make representation with GPPB for the result of their communication re: Rehabilitation/Construction of Tanauan Public Market Complex.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Management require the municipal officials to: 1. strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 in the LGU’s procurement and activities; and 2. submit complete and regular supporting documents for its transaction.
